documents.summary: Conscience and concern — two qualities that keep one from harm.

documents.title: Ekamsena Sutta
documents.subtitle: Categorically
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The drawbacks of unskillful conduct and the rewards of skillful conduct.

documents.title: Kusala Sutta
documents.subtitle: Skillful
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Yes, it really is possible to abandon unskillful habits and to develop skillful ones.

documents.title: Bala-pandita Sutta
documents.subtitle: Fools & Wise People
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: What's the difference between a fool and a wise person?

documents.title: Neyyatha Sutta
documents.subtitle: A Meaning to be Inferred
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Two short reminders not to misquote or misrepresent the Buddha's teachings.

documents.title: Vijja-bhagiya Sutta
documents.subtitle: A Share in Clear Knowing
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: How tranquillity (samatha) and insight (vipassana) function together to uproot passion and ignorance.

documents.title: Kataññu Suttas
documents.subtitle: Gratitude
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: We owe a great debt to our parents. The gratitude we show to them is a measure of our personal integrity.

documents.title: Aññataro Brāhmaṇo Sutta
documents.subtitle: A Certain Brahman
documents.author: K. Nizamis
documents.summary: The Buddha answers the question, "What do you teach?".

documents.title: Kandarayana Sutta
documents.subtitle: To Kandarayana
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: What makes a person an elder worthy of respect?

documents.title: Ukkacita Sutta
documents.subtitle: Bombast
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Two kinds of people: those who listen attentively and critically to the Dhamma, and those who listen uncritically to other teachings.

documents.title: Bala Sutta
documents.subtitle: Fools
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Two kinds of fools.

documents.title: Dullabha Sutta
documents.subtitle: Hard to Find
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: A grateful person is hard to find.

documents.title: Ghosa Suttas
documents.subtitle: Voice
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Appropriate attention (yoniso manasikara) is a condition for Right View.

documents.title: Lakkhana Sutta
documents.subtitle: Characterized (by Action)
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: How to recognize a wise person and a fool.

documents.title: Rathakara (Pacetana) Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Chariot Maker
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha recalls a previous lifetime during which he was a chariot-maker "skilled in dealing with the crookedness of wood." Now, as the Buddha, he is skilled in dealing with the crookedness of thought, word, and deed.

documents.title: Gilana Sutta
documents.subtitle: Sick People
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha compares the Dhamma to good medicine.

documents.title: Nidana Sutta
documents.subtitle: Causes
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: An action (kamma) performed by an arahant bears no kammic fruit. This sutta explains why.

documents.title: Hatthaka Sutta
documents.subtitle: To Hatthaka
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Is a comfortable home the best guarantee for a good night's sleep?

documents.title: Sukhamala Sutta
documents.subtitle: Refinement
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha describes the insights that led him as a young man to go forth, and how those insights apply to the conduct of our own lives.

documents.title: Adhipateyya Sutta
documents.subtitle: Governing Principles
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha describes three governing principles that keep one's Dhamma practice on-track. Beware: there's nowhere to hide from your unskillful actions!

documents.title: Sankhata Sutta
documents.subtitle: Fabricated
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The marks by which fabricated and unfabricated experiences can be recognized.

documents.title: Pabbata Sutta
documents.subtitle: A Mountain
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: A parent's responsibility to his or her family. If you want your family to prosper, then be like a mountain of virtue, conviction, and discernment.

documents.title: Dvejana Sutta
documents.subtitle: Two People (2)
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha offers advice to two aging brahmans who are facing the end of life.

documents.title: Vaccha Sutta
documents.subtitle: To Vaccha
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Every act of generosity is meritorious, but some are more so than others.

documents.title: Sangarava Sutta
documents.subtitle: To Sangarava
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha answers the accusation that the spiritual path he teaches is a selfish one.

documents.title: Tittha Sutta
documents.subtitle: Sectarians
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha explains how three common views about pain and pleasure can, if followed to their logical conclusion, lead to a life of inaction. He then shows how pain and pleasure actually do come about and how they can be transcended.

documents.title: Bhaya Sutta
documents.subtitle: Dangers
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Although fire, flood, and war may threaten to divide families, the world is fraught with even greater dangers. Here is a surefire way to overcome them all.

documents.title: Kalama Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Instruction to the Kalamas
documents.author: Soma Thera
documents.summary: The Buddha explains to a group of skeptics the proper criteria for accepting a spiritual teaching.

documents.title: Salha Sutta
documents.subtitle: To Salha
documents.author: Ñanamoli Thera
documents.summary: The arahant Ven. Nandaka engages the layman Salha in a dialogue that ranges from elementary principles all the way to the nature of arahantship.

documents.title: Kathavatthu Sutta
documents.subtitle: Topics for Discussion
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: This short discourse contains detailed practical instructions on how to answer questions skillfully. A valuable teaching for politicians, debaters, and the rest of us.

documents.title: Titthiya Sutta
documents.subtitle: Sectarians
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: How appropriate attention (yoniso manasikara) lies at the heart of any effort to abandon the roots of greed, hatred, and delusion.

documents.title: Mula Sutta
documents.subtitle: Roots
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: What motivates a person to wrongly imprison people and subject them to beatings? The answer lies right here, in your own heart.

documents.title: Muluposatha Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Roots of the Uposatha
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha describes to Visakha, the laywoman, right and wrong ways of observing the Uposatha days. Those who observe the Uposatha correctly are reap great rewards.

documents.title: Channa Sutta
documents.subtitle: To Channa the Wanderer
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Ven. Ananda instructs Channa on abandoning the mental defilements of passion, aversion, and delusion.

documents.title: Ajivaka Sutta
documents.subtitle: To the Fatalists' Student
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Ven. Ananda gives a skillful answer to the questions, "Whose teaching is right? Whose practice is right?"

documents.title: Sakka Sutta
documents.subtitle: To the Sakyan
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Mahanama asks the Buddha, "Which comes first: concentration or wisdom?" Ven. Ananda answers on behalf of the Buddha, who is recovering from an illness.

documents.title: Bhava Sutta
documents.subtitle: Becoming (1)
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The three levels on which becoming (bhava) operates, in relation to consciousness.

documents.title: Bhava Sutta
documents.subtitle: Becoming (2)
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The three levels on which becoming (bhava) operates, in relation to intention.

documents.title: Silabbata Sutta
documents.subtitle: Precept & Practice
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Are all religious paths fruitful? Ven. Ananda answers.

documents.title: Gadrabha Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Donkey
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Practicing the Dhamma means more than simply acting the part.

documents.title: Vajjiputta Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Vajjian Monk
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: A monk who is having difficulty following all the Pāṭimokkha training rules can boil them all down to these three.

documents.title: Sekhin Sutta
documents.subtitle: One in Training (1)
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The various levels of noble attainment, described in terms of the degree of accomplishment of each of the three trainings.

documents.title: Sekhin Sutta
documents.subtitle: One in Training (2)
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: A discussion of the various levels of noble attainment, dividing stream-winners into three types, and non-returners into five.

documents.title: Sikkha Sutta
documents.subtitle: Trainings (2)
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha summarizes the three aspects of Dhamma practice that should be developed.

documents.title: Accayika Sutta
documents.subtitle: Urgent
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Just as a farmer can't predict when the fruit will ripen, so we can't predict when Awakening will occur. So just keep your practice strong; the rest will take care of itself.

documents.title: Ajaniya Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Thoroughbred
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: What qualities make a monk worthy of respect?

documents.title: Lonaphala Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Salt Crystal
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: A Buddhist response to the question, "Why do bad things happen to good people?"

documents.title: Nimitta Sutta
documents.subtitle: Themes
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha compares the skillful training of one's mind to the way a goldsmith purifies gold ore.

documents.title: Kuta Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Peak of the Roof
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Protect your mind, and you guard yourself from harm.

documents.title: Moneyya Sutta
documents.subtitle: Sagacity
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha describes three kinds of wisdom: bodily, verbal, and mental. (This is one of the suttas selected by King Asoka (r. 270-232 BCE) to be studied and reflected upon frequently by all practicing Buddhists. See That the True Dhamma Might Last a Long Time: Readings Selected by King Asoka, by Thanissaro.)

documents.title: Gotamaka-cetiya Sutta
documents.subtitle: At Gotamaka Shrine
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: What more do you want from the Buddha's teachings?

documents.title: Katuviya Sutta
documents.subtitle: Putrid
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: When you let your concentration slip and your mindfulness falter, there's no telling what nasty sorts of flies will start swarming around you.

documents.title: Lekha Sutta
documents.subtitle: Inscriptions
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Why hold on to your anger? Beware you don't let it get carved deep into your psyche, like an inscription in solid rock.

documents.title: Dhamma-niyama Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Discourse on the Orderliness of the Dhamma
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Whether or not a Buddha arises in the world, the three characteristics of existence always remain: impermanence, stress, and not-self.

documents.title: Anubuddha Sutta
documents.subtitle: Understanding
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Why do we wander aimlessly in samsara? It's because we haven't yet realized four noble qualities.

documents.title: Anusota Sutta
documents.subtitle: With the Flow
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: A reminder that the popular advice to "just go with the flow" finds no support in the Buddha's teachings.

documents.title: Yoga Sutta
documents.subtitle: Yokes
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: In many discourses, the Buddha speaks of "the unexcelled rest from the yoke." In this discourse he explains what yokes he is referring to, and how that rest comes about.

documents.title: Agati Sutta
documents.subtitle: Off Course
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha explains the difference between staying "on course" and straying "off course" in one's Dhamma practice.

documents.title: Kalaka Sutta
documents.subtitle: At Kalaka's Park
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Even though the Buddha has deep understanding, he doesn't take a stance on any of it.

documents.title: Ariya-vamsa Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Discourse on the Traditions of the Noble Ones
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha describes four good qualities in a monk: contentment with regard to robes, almsfood, and lodging, and finding pleasure in cultivating wholesome mental states. (This is one of the suttas selected by King Asoka (r. 270-232 BCE) to be studied and reflected upon frequently by all practicing Buddhists. See That the True Dhamma Might Last a Long Time: Readings Selected by King Asoka, by Thanissaro.)

documents.title: Cakka Sutta
documents.subtitle: Wheels
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Here is one kind of four-wheel drive that is sure to keep you on the road.

documents.title: Sangaha Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Bonds of Fellowship
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The qualities that help hold together a family — or any community.

documents.title: Vassakara Sutta
documents.subtitle: With Vassakara
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Four distinguishing qualities of a wise person.

documents.title: Dona Sutta
documents.subtitle: With Dona
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: A passerby, struck by the Buddha's serene presence, asks him, "What are you? Are you a deva? A spirit? A human being?" The Buddha's now-famous reply has made this one of the most oft-quoted passages in the entire Canon.

documents.title: Aparihani Sutta
documents.subtitle: No Falling Away
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: If one is sincere in one's aspirations to realize Awakening, these four aspects of Dhamma practice should be constantly developed.

documents.title: Samadhi Sutta
documents.subtitle: Concentration
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha explains how concentration, when fully developed, can bring about any one of four different desirable results.

documents.title: Pañha Sutta
documents.subtitle: Questions
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha's teachings on skillfulness and speech extend to mastering the art of answering questions.

documents.title: Rohitassa Sutta
documents.subtitle: To Rohitassa
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: A well-traveled deva learns that we don't have to go to the ends of the world to find an end to suffering; we need look no further than right here, in this very body.

documents.title: Vipallasa Sutta
documents.subtitle: Perversions
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Four kinds of misperceptions that keep us bound to the cycle of rebirths.

documents.title: Upakkilesa Sutta
documents.subtitle: Obscurations
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Four unskillful activities that prevent a monk from shining with Dhamma.

documents.title: Samajivina Sutta
documents.subtitle: Living in Tune
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Would you like to live with your current spouse in future lives, too? Here's how.

documents.title: Anana Sutta
documents.subtitle: Debtless
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha tells the wealthy lay-follower Anathapindika about four kinds of happiness that a householder may enjoy. Some require wealth, but the best is free of charge.

documents.title: Ahina Sutta
documents.subtitle: By a Snake
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: How the practice of metta (loving-kindness) can serve as a protection against harm.

documents.title: Sappurisa Sutta
documents.subtitle: A Person of Integrity
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Are you a person of integrity? How you speak about yourself and others reveals much about your personal integrity.

documents.title: Acintita Sutta
documents.subtitle: Unconjecturable
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: If you spend too much time pondering these four things you will surely drive yourself crazy.

documents.title: Vanijja Sutta
documents.subtitle: Trade
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: One reason why some people succeed and others fail in their trades.

documents.title: Tamonata Sutta
documents.subtitle: Darkness
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: A person's goodness is measured not by his or her wealth, beauty, or status, but by the goodness of his or her actions.

documents.title: Samadhi Sutta
documents.subtitle: Concentration (Tranquillity and Insight)
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha explains how correct meditation practice consists of the development of both insight (vipassana) and tranquillity (samatha).

documents.title: Chavalata Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Firebrand
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Which is better: to practice Dhamma for one's own benefit or for another's? The answer may surprise you.

documents.title: Raga-vinaya Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Subduing of Passion
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: What does it mean to practice Dhamma for one's own benefit — and for another's?

documents.title: Sikkha Sutta
documents.subtitle: Trainings
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: It is best of all if you not only follow the precepts yourself, but can support others in following them, too.

documents.title: Valahaka Sutta
documents.subtitle: Thunderheads
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Reading suttas is good, but there is more to be done. Go meditate!

documents.title: Kesi Sutta
documents.subtitle: To Kesi the Horsetrainer
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha explains to Kesi, a horsetrainer, how he teaches Dhamma. This brilliant exposition warrants close study by every teacher, as it reveals the multiple levels in which effective teaching operates: the Buddha speaks in terms that the listener understands (horsetraining), he uses similes to great effect, and he deftly answers the real question that lies behind the student's query ("Please, can you train me?").

documents.title: Patoda Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Goad
documents.author: F.L. Woodward
documents.summary: How much dukkha does it take to motivate you to practice the Dhamma in earnest? The Buddha illustrates his point with the famous simile of a thoroughbred horse stirred to action by its rider.

documents.title: Thana Sutta
documents.subtitle: Courses of Action
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: When faced with a choice, how does one decide which course of action to follow? The Buddha here offers some helpful advice.

documents.title: Jhana Sutta
documents.subtitle: Mental Absorption (1)
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha describes four possible courses of rebirth open to someone who practices jhana.

documents.title: Jhana Sutta
documents.subtitle: Mental Absorption (2)
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha describes another possible course of rebirth open to someone who practices jhana.

documents.title: Metta Sutta
documents.subtitle: Good Will (1)
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha describes four possible courses of rebirth open to someone who practices the brahma-vihara (good will, compassion, appreciative joy, and equanimity).

documents.title: Metta Sutta
documents.subtitle: Good Will (2)
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha describes another possible course of rebirth open to someone who practices the brahma-vihara (good will, compassion, appreciative joy, and equanimity).

documents.title: Obhasa Sutta
documents.subtitle: Brightness
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: What provides the most brightness in life?

documents.title: Bhikkhuni Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Nun
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Ven. Ananda teaches a nun that, although craving can be used to overcome craving, and conceit to overcome conceit, the same principle does not hold for sexual intercourse.

documents.title: Vitthara Sutta
documents.subtitle: (Modes of Practice) in Detail
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Your native personality traits may influence how painful or pleasant your meditation practice is; but the speed of progress is determined by factors within your control.

documents.title: Asubha Sutta
documents.subtitle: Unattractiveness
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: How to make meditation practice both more pleasant and more efficient.

documents.title: Khama Sutta
documents.subtitle: Tolerant (1)
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Four modes of practice: intolerant, tolerant, self-controlled, and even.

documents.title: Khama Sutta
documents.subtitle: Tolerant (2)
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Four modes of practice: intolerant, tolerant, self-controlled, and even.

documents.title: Yuganaddha Sutta
documents.subtitle: In Tandem
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Ven. Ananda describes the paths to arahantship by which insight (vipassana) and tranquillity (samatha) work hand-in-hand.

documents.title: Kotthita Sutta
documents.subtitle: To Kotthita
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: How Sariputta answers the question, "What lies beyond Nibbana?"

documents.title: Jambali Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Waste-water Pool
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha uses some memorable similes to describe the overcoming of self-identification and ignorance.

documents.title: Nibbana Sutta
documents.subtitle: Unbinding
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Why do some people gain Awakening in this life, while others don't?

documents.title: Yodhajiva Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Warrior
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: An accomplished meditator — like a great warrior — develops these four qualities.

documents.title: Suta Sutta
documents.subtitle: On What is Heard
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Why the principle of truthfulness does not imply total frankness or openness.

documents.title: Abhaya Sutta
documents.subtitle: Fearless
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha explains to Janussonin four ways to overcome the fear of death.

documents.title: Thana Sutta
documents.subtitle: Traits
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: How can you recognize a good and wise person? The Buddha explains what qualities to look for and how to spot them.

documents.title: Tanha Sutta
documents.subtitle: Craving
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha enumerates the many kinds of tangled thoughts experienced by a mind not yet free of craving. Sound familiar?

documents.title: Pema Sutta
documents.subtitle: Affection
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The opinions of our friends and enemies often influence our own thoughts and feelings about others. This kind of thinking is rooted in craving, and the Buddha offers a cure.

documents.title: Ariyamagga Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Noble Path
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Skillful actions (kamma) eventually bring good results, while unskillful ones bring bad. But best of all are the actions that lead to the ending of kamma altogether.

documents.title: Sikkha Sutta
documents.subtitle: Training
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: This sutta makes the point that the duty of mindfulness is not simply to watch things arise and pass away. Instead, its duty is to remember to make the elements of the path arise and to prevent them from passing away.

documents.title: Pariyesana Sutta
documents.subtitle: Searches
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: What are you searching for? Are you looking for happiness in all the wrong places? Are you looking for a lasting, noble happiness?

documents.title: Kula Sutta
documents.subtitle: On Families
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: How a family loses or preserves its wealth.

documents.title: Araññika Sutta
documents.subtitle: A Wilderness Dweller
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: What sort of person is fit to live in the wilderness?

documents.title: Vitthara Sutta
documents.subtitle: (Strengths) in Detail
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: A summary of the five "strengths" (bala) to be developed in Dhamma practice.

documents.title: Hita Sutta
documents.subtitle: Benefit
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: How to practice Dhamma for the benefit of both oneself and others.

documents.title: Anuggahita Sutta
documents.subtitle: Supported
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Five factors that lead to the fulfillment of right view.

documents.title: Samadhi Sutta
documents.subtitle: (Immeasurable) Concentration
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha encourages the practice of the brahmavihara (sublime states of metta, karuna, mudita, and upekkha) as a basis for concentration practice, as it leads to five important realizations.

documents.title: Samadhanga Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Factors of Concentration
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha explains how the progressive development of jhana (absorption) leads to the development of the supranormal powers and Awakening.

documents.title: Cankama Sutta
documents.subtitle: Walking
documents.author: Aggacitta Bhikkhu & Kumara Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The benefits of walking meditation.

documents.title: Cankama Sutta
documents.subtitle: Walking
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The benefits of walking meditation.

documents.title: Nagita Sutta
documents.subtitle: To Nagita
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The raucous carryings-on of a group of brahmans lead the Buddha to reflect on the rewards of detachment.

documents.title: Siha Sutta
documents.subtitle: To General Siha (On Generosity)
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: General Siha, known for his generosity, asks the Buddha about the fruits of generosity that one can experience in this life. The Buddha describes four such fruits; a fifth (a happy rebirth) Siha can only take on faith.

documents.title: Kaladana Sutta
documents.subtitle: Seasonable Gifts
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Gifts given at the proper time bear the greatest fruit. Here the Buddha describes five such occasions. [Often chanted by monks as a blessing after receiving food or other offerings.]

documents.title: Bhojana Sutta
documents.subtitle: A Meal
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Whenever one gives the gift of food, five wonderful things are also given, automatically, to both giver and recipient alike. [Often chanted by monks as a blessing after receiving food or other offerings.]

documents.title: Saddha Sutta
documents.subtitle: Conviction
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The five rewards that a layperson can expect for having conviction (faith) in the Triple Gem.

documents.title: Adiya Sutta
documents.subtitle: Benefits to be Obtained (from Wealth)
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha describes for the wealthy householder Anathapindika five skillful ways of using one's money that bring immense benefits to the giver — benefits that last long after all the wealth is gone. [Often chanted by monks as a blessing after receiving food or other offerings.]

documents.title: Ittha Sutta
documents.subtitle: What is Welcome
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha explains to Anathapindika how true happiness can never be achieved merely by wishing for it.

documents.title: Kosala Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Kosalan
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: When Queen Mallika dies, her husband, King Pasenadi, is overcome with grief. The Buddha advises the king on how to free himself of obsessive grieving.

documents.title: Avarana Sutta
documents.subtitle: Obstacles
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha invokes a vivid simile to illustrate the hazards posed by the hindrances.

documents.title: Anga Sutta
documents.subtitle: Factors (for Exertion)
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The five factors that sustain the proper level of exertion toward the goal.

documents.title: Upajjhatthana Sutta
documents.subtitle: Subjects for Contemplation
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha describes the "five facts that one should reflect on often, whether one is a woman or a man, lay or ordained."

documents.title: Vuddhi Sutta
documents.subtitle: Growth
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: What does it mean to grow in the Dhamma?

documents.title: Dhamma-viharin Sutta
documents.subtitle: One Who Dwells in the Dhamma
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: What does it mean to be truly committed to the Dhamma?

documents.title: Yodhajiva Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Warrior (2)
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Two suttas on how a monk intent on reaching the goal must steadfastly guard his celibacy in the face of all temptation.

documents.title: Anagata-bhayani Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Discourse on Future Dangers (4)
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha reminds the monks that the practice of Dhamma should not be put off for a later date, for there are no guarantees that the future will provide any opportunities for practice. (These suttas are among those selected by King Asoka (r. 270-232 BCE) to be studied and reflected upon frequently by all practicing Buddhists. See That the True Dhamma Might Last a Long Time: Readings Selected by King Asoka, by Thanissaro.)

documents.subtitle: Second Discourse on the Pleasant
documents.author: K. Nizamis
documents.summary: The benefits of delighting in the Dhamma-Vinaya.

documents.title: Kathavatthu Sutta
documents.subtitle: Topics of Conversation (1)
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Ten wholesome topics of conversation as an alternative to gossip.

documents.title: Kathavatthu Sutta
documents.subtitle: Topics of Conversation (2)
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Right speech is most praiseworthy when you embody the good things you talk about.

documents.title: Akankha Sutta
documents.subtitle: Wishes
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: This discourse lists ten reasons, in ascending worth, for perfecting the precepts and being committed to the development of calm (samatha) and insight (vipassana). An interesting feature of this discussion is that the Buddha does not separate insight and jhana into separate paths of practice, and actually cites insight, together with tranquillity, as a prerequisite for mastering the four jhanas.

documents.title: Aghata Sutta
documents.subtitle: Hatred
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: When hatred arises in the mind what do you do? Here are ten possible antidotes.

documents.title: Bahuna Sutta
documents.subtitle: To Bahuna
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Of what is an Awakened being freed?

documents.title: Vera Sutta
documents.subtitle: Animosity
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: What it takes for a lay person to become a stream-winner.

documents.title: Ditthi Sutta
documents.subtitle: Views
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The householder Anathapindika instructs a group of non-Buddhist wanderers on the nature of Right View.

documents.title: Vajjiya Sutta
documents.subtitle: About Vajjiya
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: A lay disciple answers the charge that the Buddha doesn't have any straightforward teachings.

documents.title: Uttiya Sutta
documents.subtitle: To Uttiya
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Is the goal of the Buddha's teachings to liberate all beings?

documents.title: Kokanuda Sutta
documents.subtitle: To Kokanuda
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Ven. Ananda explains that wisdom is not based on subscribing to this or that point of view.

documents.title: Micchatta Sutta
documents.subtitle: Wrongness
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Success or failure on the Path hinges on the extent to which one has right view.

documents.title: Bija Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Seed
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Two similes illustrate how success or failure on the Path hinges on right view.

documents.title: Virecana (Tikicchaka) Sutta
documents.subtitle: A Purgative
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Sometimes even the best medicines for the body don't work. Here, the Buddha offers a "noble purgative" for the mind that never fails.

documents.title: Orimatīra Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Near Shore
documents.author: K. Nizamis
documents.summary: What is the near shore? What is the far shore?

documents.title: Cunda Kammaraputta Sutta
documents.subtitle: To Cunda the Silversmith
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha explains to Cunda that genuine self-purification comes about not from performing sacred rites, but by cultivating skillfulness in one's thoughts, words, and deeds.

documents.title: Janussonin Sutta
documents.subtitle: To Janussonin
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Who stands to benefit most from a gift given in another's honor?

documents.title: Brahmavihara Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Sublime Attitudes
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha's instructions on the practice and rewards of the four sublime attitudes: metta (goodwill, loving-kindness), karuna (compassion), mudita (appreciative joy), and upekkha (equanimity).

documents.title: Kimattha Sutta
documents.subtitle: What is the Purpose?
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Why does the Buddha repeatedly implore us to cultivate sila (virtue)?

documents.title: Cetana Sutta
documents.subtitle: An Act of Will
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Good qualities in the heart naturally lead to the development of other good qualities. And it all starts with sila (virtue).

documents.title: Sandha Sutta
documents.subtitle: To Sandha
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: What makes the arahant's experience of jhana unique?

documents.title: Mahanama Sutta
documents.subtitle: To Mahanama (1)
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha instructs the householder Mahanama on the importance of developing the six recollections (recollection of the Buddha, Dhamma, Sangha, one's own virtues, one's own generosity, and the devas).

documents.title: Mahanama Sutta
documents.subtitle: To Mahanama (2)
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha further instructs the householder Mahanama on the importance of developing the six recollections, reminding him to develop these recollections in every posture, even "while you are busy at work, while you are resting in your home crowded with children."

documents.title: Metta (Mettanisamsa) Sutta
documents.subtitle: Good Will
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Eleven benefits arising from the practice of metta (loving kindness, or goodwill) meditation.

documents.title: Dasama Sutta
documents.subtitle: To Dasama
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Ven. Ananda describes eleven modes of practice that can lead to the Deathless. (This sutta is identical to MN 52, but without the preamble.)

documents.title: Gopalaka Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Cowherd
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Eleven factors that are conducive to spiritual growth, and eleven that are obstructive. (This sutta is identical to MN 33, but without the preamble.)

documents.title: Brahmajāla Sutta
documents.subtitle: The All-embracing Net of Views
documents.author: Bhikkhu Bodhi
documents.summary: In this important sutta, the first in the Tipitaka, the Buddha describes sixty-two philosophical and speculative views concerning the self and the world that were prevalent among spiritual seekers of his day. In rejecting these teachings — many of which thrive to this day — he decisively establishes the parameters of his own.

documents.title: Samaññaphala Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Fruits of the Contemplative Life
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: King Ajatasattu asks the Buddha, "What are the fruits of the contemplative life, visible in the here and now?" The Buddha replies by painting a comprehensive portrait of the Buddhist path of training, illustrating each stage of the training with vivid similes.

documents.title: Potthapada Sutta
documents.subtitle: About Potthapada
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The wandering ascetic Potthapada brings to the Buddha a tangle of questions concerning the nature of perception. The Buddha clears up the matter by reviewing the fundamentals of concentration meditation and showing how it can lead to the ultimate cessation of perception.

documents.title: Kevatta (Kevaddha) Sutta
documents.subtitle: To Kevatta
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: This discourse explores the role of miracles and conversations with heavenly beings as a possible basis for faith and belief. The Buddha does not deny the reality of such experiences, but he points out that — of all possible miracles — the only reliable one is the miracle of instruction in the proper training of the mind. As for heavenly beings, they are subject to greed, anger, and delusion, and so the information they give — especially with regard to the miracle of instruction — is not necessarily trustworthy. Thus the only valid basis for faith is the instruction that, when followed, brings about the end of one's own mental defilements. The tale that concludes the discourse is one of the finest examples of the early Buddhist sense of humor.

documents.title: Lohicca Sutta
documents.subtitle: To Lohicca
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: A non-Buddhist poses some good questions: If Dhamma is something that one must realize for oneself, then what is the role of a teacher? Are there any teachers who don't deserve some sort of criticism? The Buddha's reply includes a sweeping summary of the entire path of practice.

documents.title: Maha-nidana Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Great Causes Discourse
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: One of the most profound discourses in the Pali canon, which gives an extended treatment of the teachings of dependent co-arising (paticca samuppada) and not-self (anatta) in an outlined context of how these teachings function in practice. An explanatory preface is included.

documents.title: Maha-parinibbana Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Great Discourse on the Total Unbinding
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: This wide-ranging sutta, the longest one in the Pali canon, describes the events leading up to, during, and immediately following the death and final release (parinibbana) of the Buddha. This colorful narrative contains a wealth of Dhamma teachings, including the Buddha's final instructions that defined how Buddhism would be lived and practiced long after the Buddha's death — even to this day. But this sutta also depicts, in simple language, the poignant human drama that unfolds among the Buddha's many devoted followers around the time of the death of their beloved teacher.

documents.title: Maha-samaya Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Great Meeting
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: A large group of devas pays a visit to the Buddha. This sutta is the closest thing in the Pali canon to a "Who's Who" of the deva worlds, providing useful material for anyone interested in the cosmology of early Buddhism.

documents.title: Sakka-pañha Sutta
documents.subtitle: Sakka's Questions
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Sakka, the deva-king, asks the Buddha about the sources of conflict, and about the path of practice that can bring it to an end. This discourse ends with a humorous account about Sakka's frustration in trying to learn the Dhamma from other contemplatives. It's hard to find a teacher when you're a king.

documents.title: Maha-satipatthana Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Great Frames of Reference
documents.author: Burma Piṭaka Association
documents.summary: This sutta offers comprehensive practical instructions on the development of mindfulness in meditation. The Buddha describes how the development of continuous mindfulness of the four satipatthana ("foundations of mindfulness" or "frames of reference") — mindfulness of the body, of feelings, of the mind, and of mind-objects — can lead ultimately to full Awakening. [The text of this sutta is identical to that of the Satipatthana Sutta (MN 10), except that the Majjhima version omits the exposition of the Four Noble Truths (sections 5a,b,c and d in part D of this version).]

documents.title: Maha-satipatthana Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Great Frames of Reference
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: This sutta offers comprehensive practical instructions on the development of mindfulness in meditation. The Buddha describes how the development of continuous mindfulness of the four satipatthana ("foundations of mindfulness" or "frames of reference") — mindfulness of the body, of feelings, of the mind, and of mind-objects — can lead ultimately to full Awakening. [The text of this sutta is identical to that of the Satipatthana Sutta (MN 10), except that the Majjhima version omits the exposition of the Four Noble Truths (sections 5a,b,c and d in part D of this version).]

documents.title: Cakkavatti Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Wheel-turning Emperor
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: In this excerpt the Buddha explains how skillful action can result in the best kind of long life, the best kind of beauty, the best kind of happiness, and the best kind of strength.

documents.title: Sigalovada Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Buddha's Advice to Sigalaka
documents.author: John Kelly, Sue Sawyer, and Victoria Yareham
documents.summary: The householder's code of discipline, as described by the Buddha to the layman Sigala. This sutta offers valuable practical advice for householders on how to conduct themselves skillfully in their relationships with parents, spouses, children, pupils, teachers, employers, employees, friends, and spiritual mentors so as to bring happiness to all concerned.

documents.title: Sigalovada Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Discourse to Sigala
documents.author: Narada Thera
documents.summary: The householder's code of discipline, as described by the Buddha to the layman Sigala. This sutta offers valuable practical advice for householders on how to conduct themselves skillfully in their relationships with parents, spouses, children, pupils, teachers, employers, employees, friends, and spiritual mentors so as to bring happiness to all concerned.

documents.title: Atanatiya Sutta
documents.subtitle: Discourse on Atanatiya
documents.author: Piyadassi Thera
documents.summary: One of the "protective verses" (paritta) that are chanted to this day for ceremonial purposes by Theravada monks and nuns around the world. See Piyadassi Thera's The Book of Protection.

documents.title: The Dhammapada
documents.subtitle: The Buddha's Path of Wisdom
documents.author: Acharya Buddharakkhita
documents.summary: An anthology of two Dhammapada translations: one by Acharya Buddharakkhita, one by Thanissaro Bhikkhu.

documents.title: The Dhammapada
documents.subtitle: A Translation
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Dhammapada, an anthology of verses attributed to the Buddha, has long been recognized as one of the masterpieces of early Buddhist literature. Only more recently have scholars realized that it is also one of the early masterpieces in the Indian tradition of kavya, or belles lettres. This translation of the Dhammapada is an attempt to render the verses into English in a way that does justice to both of the traditions to which the text belongs. This translation also grew from the translator's conviction that the text deserved to be offered freely as a gift of Dhamma. As few good-quality translations of this beloved text are currently available as gifts, the translator was inspired to make his own. Thus was this translation born.

documents.title: Itivuttaka
documents.subtitle: This Was Said by the Buddha
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu

documents.title: Itivuttaka
documents.subtitle: This Was Said by the Buddha
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu

documents.title: The Healing Medicine of the Dhamma
documents.author: Andrew Olendzki

documents.title: Guhatthaka-suttaniddeso
documents.subtitle: Upon the Tip of a Needle
documents.author: Andrew Olendzki
documents.summary: In a series of verses of stunning simplicity and elegance, Sariputta describes the fleeting evanescence of the present moment.

documents.title: Tirokudda Kanda
documents.subtitle: Hungry Shades Outside the Walls
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Some of your ancestors and deceased loved ones may have been reborn as hungry ghosts, no longer able to fend for themselves. In this poem the Buddha explains that it is to your long-term benefit — and to theirs — that you honor their memory with gifts.

documents.title: Uraga Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Snake
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: One who advances far along the path sheds unwholesome states of mind, as a snake sheds its dried up old skin.

documents.title: Dhaniya Sutta
documents.subtitle: Dhaniya the Cattleman
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: A poetic dialogue contrasting the wealth and security of lay life with the wealth and security of a person who has lived the renunciate life to its culmination. If you have trouble relating to someone like Dhaniya who measures his wealth in cattle, then when reading this poem substitute stocks and bonds for cows and bulls, and economic downturn for rain.

documents.title: Khaggavisana Sutta
documents.subtitle: A Rhinoceros
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: On the value of living the solitary wandering life of a forest monk.

documents.title: Kasi Bharadvaja Sutta
documents.subtitle: To the Plowing Bharadvaja
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha answers a farmer who asserts that monks do no useful work, and thus don't deserve to eat.

documents.title: Cunda Sutta
documents.subtitle: To Cunda
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Four different types of contemplatives and how to recognize them.

documents.title: Parabhava Sutta
documents.subtitle: Discourse on Downfall
documents.author: Piyadassi Thera
documents.summary: On the various causes of spiritual decline that the aspirant must avoid.

documents.title: Vasala Sutta
documents.subtitle: Discourse on Outcasts
documents.author: Piyadassi Thera
documents.summary: The Buddha explains to a brahman what qualities really make one worthy of being branded an "outcast."

documents.title: Karaniya Metta Sutta
documents.subtitle: Good Will
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha's words on cultivating a heart filled with loving-kindness (metta) towards all beings.

documents.title: Alavaka Sutta
documents.subtitle: To the Alavaka Yakkha
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: A yakkha challenges the Buddha with riddles and threatens to beat him up.

documents.title: Vijaya Sutta
documents.subtitle: Victory
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Reflecting on the unattractiveness of the body as a way to gain insight.

documents.title: Muni Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Sage
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha describes the characteristics of the ideal sage, who finds greater happiness and security not in relationships but in living the solitary contemplative life. (This is one of the suttas selected by King Asoka (r. 270-232 BCE) to be studied and reflected upon frequently by all practicing Buddhists. See That the True Dhamma Might Last a Long Time: Readings Selected by King Asoka, by Thanissaro Bhikkhu.)

documents.title: Ratana Sutta
documents.subtitle: Treasures
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha enumerates the many treasures to be found within the Triple Gem.

documents.title: Hiri Sutta
documents.subtitle: Conscience
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: What is a true friend?

documents.title: Maha-mangala Sutta
documents.subtitle: Protection
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: An enumeration of the blessings that result from leading a skillful life.

documents.title: Dhammacariya Sutta
documents.subtitle: Wrong Conduct
documents.author: John D. Ireland
documents.summary: The monks are encouraged to avoid monks who conduct their lives in unwholesome ways.

documents.title: Nava Sutta
documents.subtitle: A Boat
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: A teacher, like a skilled boatman, is one who knows firsthand how to cross to the opposite shore.

documents.title: Kimsila Sutta
documents.subtitle: With What Virtue?
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The attitudes and behavior that enable one best to learn and benefit from the Dhamma.

documents.title: Utthana Sutta
documents.subtitle: Initiative
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: A stirring exhortation to rekindle your efforts. Wake up!

documents.title: Rahula Sutta
documents.subtitle: Advice to Rahula
documents.author: John D. Ireland
documents.summary: The Buddha recommends the recluse way of life to his son, Rahula.

documents.title: Dhammika Sutta
documents.subtitle: Dhammika
documents.author: John D. Ireland
documents.summary: A lay follower asks the Buddha how a disciple should act virtuously. The Buddha explains.

documents.title: Pabbaja Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Going Forth
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: King Bimbisara, struck by the young Buddha's radiant demeanor, follows him to the mountains to discover who he is and whence he comes.

documents.title: Padhana Sutta
documents.subtitle: Exertion
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The ten armies of Mara approach the Bodhisatta (Buddha-to-be) in an unsuccessful attempt to lure him from his meditation seat.

documents.title: Subhasita Sutta
documents.subtitle: Well-Spoken
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Four characteristics of well-spoken speech.

documents.title: Salla Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Arrow
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Death and loss are inevitable, but is grief?

documents.title: Nalaka Sutta
documents.subtitle: To Nalaka
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: A sutta in two parts. The first part gives an account of events soon after the birth of the Bodhisatta (Buddha-to-be). The second part describes the way of the sage.

documents.title: Dvayatanupassana Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Contemplation of Dualities
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Not all dualities are misleading. This sutta teaches ways to contemplate the duality of the origination and cessation of stress and suffering so as to reach Awakening.

documents.title: Kama Sutta
documents.subtitle: Sensual Pleasure
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The drawbacks of sensual desires.

documents.title: Guhatthaka Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Cave of the Body
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Those who remain attached to the body and to sensuality will have a hard time freeing themselves from fear of death and from further becoming.

documents.title: Dutthatthaka Sutta
documents.subtitle: Corrupted
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Freedom isn't to be found by boasting of your precepts and practices or by debating your views.

documents.title: Suddhatthaka Sutta
documents.subtitle: Pure
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Although freedom is found by means of knowledge and meditation, in ultimate terms it lies beyond both.

documents.title: Paramatthaka Sutta
documents.subtitle: Supreme
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The conceit that comes from identifying with practices or views — even if they're supreme — is a fetter preventing full freedom.

documents.title: Jara Sutta
documents.subtitle: Old Age
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Life is short. Possessiveness brings grief. Freedom comes from abandoning any sense of mine.

documents.title: Tissa Metteyya Sutta
documents.subtitle: Tissa Metteyya
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The drawbacks of falling away from the celibate life.

documents.title: Pasura Sutta
documents.subtitle: To Pasura
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha points out the drawbacks of disputes, for winners and losers, alike.

documents.title: Magandiya Sutta
documents.subtitle: To Magandiya
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Magandiya offers the Buddha his daughter in marriage. The Buddha refuses and further subdues Magandiya's pride by describing  attainments of purity that Magandiya can't yet understand.

documents.title: Purabheda Sutta
documents.subtitle: Before the Break-up of the Body
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: What enables a person to live at peace?

documents.title: Kalaha-vivada Sutta
documents.subtitle: Quarrels & Disputes
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha is questioned on the source of quarrels and disputes, and on the highest level of spiritual attainment.

documents.title: Cula-viyuha Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Lesser Array
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: If there is one truth, how should a person behave in a world where many different truths are taught?

documents.title: Maha-viyuha Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Great Array
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: How to maintain freedom in a world full of disputes.

documents.title: Tuvataka Sutta
documents.subtitle: Quickly
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: A detailed description of the attitudes and behavior of a monk training for the sake of total liberation.

documents.title: Attadanda Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Rod Embraced
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha speaks in poignant terms of the samvega that led him to abandon the home life. He concludes with recommendations for practice and a description of the person who has attained the goal of true peace and security.

documents.title: Sariputta Sutta
documents.subtitle: To Sariputta
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: When a monk, disaffected with the world, takes up the life of seclusion, what fears should he overcome? How should he train to annihilate the darkness in his heart?

documents.title: Ajita-manava-puccha
documents.subtitle: Ajita's Questions
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha summarizes the essence of Dhamma training: "Not craving for sensual pleasures, and with a mind that is pure and tranquil."

documents.title: Tissa-metteyya-manava-puccha
documents.subtitle: Tissa-metteyya's Questions
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Who in the world is truly contented, truly free, truly a great person?

documents.title: Punnaka-manava-puccha
documents.subtitle: Punnaka's Questions
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha explains that birth and aging can never be transcended by performing hopeful rituals, but only by extinguishing the fires of greed, hatred, and delusion.

documents.title: Mettagu-manava-puccha
documents.subtitle: Mettagu's Questions
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: How does one cross the flood of birth and old age, sorrow and grief?

documents.title: Dhotaka-manava-puccha
documents.subtitle: Dhotaka's Questions
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: How can one become freed of all doubt?

documents.title: Upasiva-manava-puccha
documents.subtitle: Upasiva's Questions
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: What support should one hold on to in order to cross over the raging flood of craving?

documents.title: Nanda-manava-puccha
documents.subtitle: Nanda's Questions
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Who, exactly, deserves to be called "wise"?

documents.title: Hemaka-manava-puccha
documents.subtitle: Hemaka's Question
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: How can we demolish craving and free ourselves from entanglement with the world?

documents.title: Todeyya-manava-puccha
documents.subtitle: Todeyya's Questions
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: So — what's it like, being emancipated, anyway?

documents.title: Kappa-manava-puccha
documents.subtitle: Kappa's Question
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Is there anywhere safe to stand where we won't be swept away by aging and death?

documents.title: Jatukanni-manava-puccha
documents.subtitle: Jatukannin's Question
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: How does one abandon birth and aging?

documents.title: Bhadravudha-manava-puccha
documents.subtitle: Bhadravudha's Questions
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Bhadravudha asks of the Buddha: How did you come to know the Dhamma?

documents.title: Udaya-manava-puccha
documents.subtitle: Udaya's Questions
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: In what way should one live mindfully, so as to bring about Awakening?

documents.title: Posala-manava-puccha
documents.subtitle: Posala's Questions
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: How does one develop insight after mastering the higher levels of jhana?

documents.title: Mogharaja-manava-puccha
documents.subtitle: Mogharaja's Question
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: How should one view the world so as to escape Death's grasp?

documents.title: Pingiya-manava-puccha
documents.subtitle: Pingiya's Question
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Alarmed by the deterioration of his aging body, Pingiya asks the Buddha how to conquer birth and decay.

documents.title: Heraññakani
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The results of evil deeds will catch up with you.

documents.title: Mahakala
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: May I never lie with my head cracked open again!

documents.title: Valliya
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Through persistence I shall reach the goal!

documents.title: Punnamasa
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Shed the five hindrances, and what's left?

documents.title: Nandaka
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Like a fine thoroughbred steed.

documents.title: Kanhadinna
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: No more passion for becoming.

documents.title: Sona Potiriyaputta
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Better to die in battle than to survive, defeated.

documents.title: Culaka
documents.subtitle: The Call of the Peacocks
documents.author: Andrew Olendzki
documents.summary: The beauty of the wilderness; the beauty of a heart that's free.

documents.title: Matangaputta

documents.summary: A monk takes a wrong turn and gets mugged.

documents.title: Visākhā Sutta
documents.subtitle: Visākhā
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The laywoman Visakha, grieving over the death of a grandchild, receives a powerful teaching concerning clinging and death.

documents.title: Dabba Sutta
documents.subtitle: About Dabba Mallaputta (2)
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha is inspired by Ven. Dabba Mallaputta's spectacular death and attainment of Parinibbana.

documents.title: Sirima
documents.subtitle: Sirima's Mansion
documents.author: John D. Ireland
documents.summary: The deva Sirima, an entertainer in her previous human birth, explains to Ven. Vangisa how she learned the Dhamma and became a stream-enterer.

documents.title: Sesavati
documents.subtitle: Sesavati's Mansion
documents.author: John D. Ireland
documents.summary: The deva Sesavati tells Ven. Vangisa how paying her respects to Ven. Sariputta in her previous life was enough to ensure rebirth in a glorious deva realm. This is an example of early Buddhist poetry with a purely devotional message.

documents.title: An Index to the Majjhima Nikaya
documents.author: Bhikkhu Kumara & Tahn Varado with revisions by Khemaratana Bhikkhu

documents.title: Mulapariyaya Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Root Sequence
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: In this difficult but important sutta the Buddha reviews in depth one of the most fundamental principles of Buddhist thought and practice: namely, that there is no thing — not even Nibbana itself — that can rightly be regarded as the source from which all phenomena and experience emerge.

documents.title: Sabbasava Sutta
documents.subtitle: All the Fermentations
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha teaches seven methods for eliminating from the mind the deeply rooted defilements (sensuality, becoming, views, and ignorance) that obstruct the realization of Awakening.

documents.title: Bhaya-bherava Sutta
documents.subtitle: Fear & Terror
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: What would it take to live in solitude in the wilderness, completely free of fear? The Buddha explains.

documents.title: Vatthupama Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Simile of the Cloth
documents.author: Nyanaponika Thera
documents.summary: With a simple simile the Buddha illustrates the difference between a defiled mind and a pure mind.

documents.title: Sallekha Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Discourse on Effacement
documents.author: Nyanaponika Thera
documents.summary: The Buddha explains how unskillful qualities in the heart can be eradicated through meditation.

documents.title: Sammaditthi Sutta
documents.subtitle: Right View
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: How the four noble truths, dependent co-arising, and the knowledge that ends mental fermentation all build upon the basic dichotomy between skillful and unskillful action.

documents.title: Satipatthana Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Discourse on the Arousing of Mindfulness
documents.author: Soma Thera

documents.title: Satipatthana Sutta
documents.subtitle: Frames of Reference
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha's comprehensive practical instructions on the development of mindfulness as the basis for insight. [The text of this sutta is identical to that of the Maha-satipatthana Sutta (DN 22), but without its detailed exposition of the Four Noble Truths (sections 5a,b,c and d in part D of that version).]

documents.title: Cula-sihanada Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Shorter Discourse on the Lion's Roar
documents.author: Ñanamoli Thera & Bhikkhu Bodhi
documents.summary: The Buddha declares that only through practicing in accord with the Dhamma can Awakening be realized. His teaching is distinguished from those of other religions and philosophies through its unique rejection of all doctrines of self.

documents.title: Maha-sihanada Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Great Discourse on the Lion's Roar
documents.author: Ñanamoli Thera & Bhikkhu Bodhi
documents.summary: The Buddha expounds the ten powers of a Tathagata, his four kinds of intrepidity, and other superior qualities which entitle him to "roar his lion's roar in the assemblies."

documents.title: Maha-dukkhakkhandha Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Great Mass of Stress
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: In deliciously graphic terms, the Buddha describes the allures and drawbacks of sensuality, physical form, and feeling. What better incentive could there be to escape samsara once and for all?

documents.title: Cula-dukkhakkhandha Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Lesser Mass of Stress
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: What mental qualities must be abandoned in order to free oneself of greed, aversion, and delusion? Can painful austerities be used to purify oneself and burn away the karmic fruit of past misdeeds? Through  question-and-answer dialogues with the lay follower Mahanama and with a group of Jain ascetics, the Buddha lays these questions to rest.

documents.title: Madhupindika Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Ball of Honey
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: A man looking to pick a fight asks the Buddha to explain his doctrine. The Buddha's answer mystifies not only the man, but also a number of monks. Ven. Maha Kaccana finally provides an explanation, and in the course of doing so explains what is needed to bring the psychological sources of conflict to an end.

documents.title: Dvedhavitakka Sutta
documents.subtitle: Two Sorts of Thinking
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha recounts the events leading up to his Awakening, and describes his discovery that thoughts connected with sensuality, ill-will, and harmfulness do not lead one to Awakening, while those connected with their opposites (renunciation, non ill-will, and harmlessness) do.

documents.title: Vitakkasanthana Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Relaxation of Thoughts
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha offers five practical methods of responding wisely to unskillful thoughts (thoughts connected with desire, aversion, or delusion).

documents.title: Kakacupama Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Simile of the Saw
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha tells the story of a wise slave who deliberately tests her mistress's patience. The Buddha invokes several memorable similes here to illustrate the correct way to develop patience.

documents.title: Alagaddupama Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Water-Snake Simile
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Using two famous similes, the Buddha shows how the development of right view calls for the skillful application both of grasping and of letting-go. The sutta includes one of the Canon's most important expositions on the topic of not-self.

documents.title: Ratha-vinita Sutta
documents.subtitle: Relay Chariots
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Using the simile of a set of relay chariots, Ven. Punna Mantaniputta explains the relationship of the factors of the path to the goal of the holy life.

documents.title: Ariyapariyesana Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Noble Search
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Most of us spend a good part of our lives looking for happiness in all the wrong places. In this sutta the Buddha recounts the story of his own search and points out where a true and lasting happiness can be found.

documents.title: Cula-hatthipadopama Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Shorter Elephant Footprint Simile
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: At what point do you know for sure that the Buddha's awakening was genuine?

documents.title: Maha-hatthipadopama Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Great Elephant Footprint Simile
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: An explanation of the four noble truths, focusing on the aggregate of physical form and showing (1) how all the aggregates are interrelated and (2) how all four noble truths, together with the principle of dependent co-arising, are related to the aggregates.

documents.title: Maha Saropama Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Longer Heartwood-simile Discourse
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha compares the rewards of the practice to different parts of a large tree, with total release the most valuable part of the tree: the heartwood.

documents.title: Cula Saropama Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Shorter Heartwood-simile Discourse
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha compares the rewards of the practice to different parts of a large tree, with total release the most valuable part of the tree: the heartwood.

documents.title: Maha-gopalaka Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Greater Cowherd Discourse
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Eleven factors that are conducive to spiritual growth, and eleven that are obstructive. (Apart from the preamble, this sutta is identical to AN 11.18.)

documents.title: Culagopalika Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Shorter Discourse on the Cowherd
documents.author: Andrew Olendzki
documents.summary: In this brief excerpt the Buddha urges his monks to cross over to the lasting safety of Nibbana.

documents.title: Cula-Saccaka Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Shorter Discourse to Saccaka
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Even though the Buddha did not usually seek debates, he knew how to reply effectively when attacked. In this discourse, he gets Saccaka — who uses a variety of cheap debater's tricks — to trip over those tricks. However, the Buddha goes beyond simply defeating Saccaka in debate. He then takes the opportunity to teach him the Dhamma.

documents.title: Maha-Saccaka Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Longer Discourse to Saccaka
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: In response to an insinuating remark — that his ability not to be overcome by pleasure and pain is due simply to the fact that he never experienced any intense pleasures or pains — the Buddha recounts the pains he endured in his austerities, and the pleasures that attended the path to and his attainment of Awakening.

documents.title: Mahatanhasankhaya Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Greater Craving-Destruction Discourse
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: A long discourse in which the Buddha discusses how to understand the role of consciousness — as a process — in the process of birth in a way that actually can lead to the end of birth.

documents.title: Maha-Assapura Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Greater Discourse at Assapura
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha outlines the full course of training by which a meditator may earn the right to call him- or herself a true contemplative. As presented here, the training begins with conscience and concern for the results of one's actions, and leads progressively through the cultivation of virtue, sense-restraint, moderation, wakefulness, mindfulness, alertness, the four jhanas, finally culminating in the realization of the insight knowledges.

documents.title: Saleyyaka Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Brahmans of Sala
documents.author: Ñanamoli Thera
documents.summary: A discussion of ten types of skillful and unskillful conduct in body, speech, and mind, and of the future rewards open to those who follow the guidelines to skillful conduct.

documents.title: Saleyyaka Sutta
documents.subtitle: (Brahmans) of Sala
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: A discussion of ten types of skillful and unskillful conduct in body, speech, and mind, and of the future rewards open to those who follow the guidelines to skillful conduct.

documents.title: Mahavedalla Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Greater Set of Questions-and-Answers
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Ven. Sariputta answers questions dealing with discernment, right view, and the higher meditative attainments.

documents.title: Culavedalla Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Shorter Set of Questions-and-Answers
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Dhammadinna the nun fields a series of Dhamma questions put to her by her former husband: questions on self-identification, cessation, penetration into the true nature of feeling, and the attainment of Nibbana.

documents.title: Cula-dhammasamadana Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Shorter Discourse on Taking on Practices
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Is something right because it feels right?

documents.title: Brahma-nimantanika Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Brahma Invitation
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha disarms two powerful antagonists through his profound understanding of the nature of consciousness.

documents.title: Atthakanagara Sutta
documents.subtitle: To the Man from Atthakanagara
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Ven. Ananda describes eleven modes of practice that can lead to the Deathless. (Apart from the preamble, this sutta is identical to AN 11.17.)

documents.title: Sekha-patipada Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Practice for One in Training
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: "Consummate in clear-knowing and conduct" is a standard epithet for the Buddha. This sutta explains what it means, and shows that it can be used to describe an arahant as well.

documents.title: Potaliya Sutta
documents.subtitle: To Potaliya
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Using seven graphic similes for the drawbacks of sensual passions, the Buddha teaches Potaliya the householder what it means, in the discipline of a noble one, to have entirely cut off one's worldly affairs.

documents.title: Kukkuravatika Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Dog-duty Ascetic
documents.author: Ñanamoli Thera
documents.summary: Act like a dog, and that's what you'll become. The moral: choose your actions with care.

documents.title: Abhaya Sutta
documents.subtitle: To Prince Abhaya
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha explains the criteria for determining whether or not something is worth saying. This discourse is a beautiful example of the Buddha's skill as teacher: not only does he talk about right speech, but he also demonstrates right speech in action.

documents.title: Bahuvedaniya Sutta
documents.subtitle: Many Things to be Experienced
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha discusses the range of possible pleasures and joys, and concludes by advocating a pleasure that goes beyond feeling. [The text of this sutta is almost identical to that of SN 36.19.]

documents.title: Apannaka  Sutta
documents.subtitle: A Safe Bet
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha explains to a group of householders how to navigate skillfully through the maze of wrong views.

documents.title: Ambalatthika-rahulovada Sutta
documents.subtitle: Instructions to Rahula at Mango Stone
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha admonishes his son, the novice Rahula, on the dangers of lying and stresses the importance of constant reflection on one's motives. (This is one of the suttas selected by King Asoka (r. 270-232 BCE) to be studied and reflected upon frequently by all practicing Buddhists. See That the True Dhamma Might Last a Long Time: Readings Selected by King Asoka, by Thanissaro Bhikkhu.)

documents.title: Maha-Rahulovada Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Greater Exhortation to Rahula
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha delivers meditation instructions to his son, the novice Rahula.

documents.title: Cula-Malunkyovada Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Shorter Instructions to Malunkya
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Ven. Malunkyaputta threatens to disrobe unless the Buddha answers all his speculative metaphysical questions. Using the famous simile of a man shot by a poison arrow, the Buddha reminds him that some questions are simply not worth asking.

documents.title: Latukikopama Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Quail Simile
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Fetters are strong, not because of their own tensile strength, but because of the tenacity of our unwillingness to let them go.

documents.title: Kitagiri Sutta
documents.subtitle: At Kitagiri
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: A discourse on the importance of conviction in the Buddhist path. Not only is conviction a prerequisite for listening to the Buddha's teachings with respect, but — as is shown by the unusual discussion here categorizing the types of noble disciples — it can underlie the practice all the way to the Deathless.

documents.title: Aggi-Vacchagotta Sutta
documents.subtitle: To Vacchagotta on Fire
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha explains to a wanderer why he does not hold any speculative views. Using the simile of an extinguished fire he illustrates the destiny of the liberated being.

documents.title: Dighanaka Sutta
documents.subtitle: To LongNails
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: A discussion of how to abandon doctrinaire views of radical acceptance, radical rejection, and any combination of the two.

documents.title: Magandiya Sutta
documents.subtitle: To Magandiya
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: In this passage, the Buddha teaches a member of a hedonist sect about the nature of true pleasure and true health.

documents.title: Samana-Mundika Sutta
documents.subtitle: Mundika the Contemplative
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The highest attainment is not simply the abandoning of unskillful actions and a reversion to childlike harmlessness. It requires first developing skillful habits and skillful resolves, and then letting them go.

documents.title: Ratthapala Sutta
documents.subtitle: About Ratthapala
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
