documents.summary: A two-part story about the monk who, the Buddha said, was foremost among his disciples in ordaining on the power of pure conviction. In the first part of the story, Ratthapala deals with his parents' opposition to his ordaining, and their attempts, after ordination, to lure him back to lay life. In the second part, he recalls the four observations about the world that inspired him, as a healthy and wealthy young man, to ordain in the first place.

documents.title: Angulimala Sutta
documents.subtitle: About Angulimala
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: A murderous bandit takes refuge in the Buddha, develops a heart of compassion, and becomes an arahant.

documents.title: Piyajatika Sutta
documents.subtitle: From One Who Is Dear
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: King Pasenadi of Kosala figures prominently in many discourses as a devout follower of the Buddha. In this discourse we learn how — thanks to Queen Mallika's astuteness — the king first became favorably disposed toward the Buddha.

documents.title: Kannakatthala Sutta
documents.subtitle: At Kannakatthala
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: A case study in how social advantages can be a spiritual liability. The discussion focuses on the factors needed for release — attainable by all people, regardless of caste or race — while the gently satirical frame story shows how the life of a king, or any highly placed person, presents obstacles to developing those factors.

documents.title: Assalayana Sutta
documents.subtitle: With Assalayana
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha enters into a debate with a brahman on whether one's worth as a person is determined by birth or by behavior. Although some of the arguments he presents here deal with the specifics of brahman caste pride, many of them are applicable to issues of racism and nationalism in general.

documents.title: Canki Sutta
documents.subtitle: With Canki
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: A pompous brahman teenager questions the Buddha about safeguarding, awakening to, and attaining the truth. In the course of his answer, the Buddha describes the criteria for choosing a reliable teacher and how best to learn from such a person.

documents.title: Dhanañjani Sutta
documents.subtitle: To Dhanañjanin
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: A poignant story of a lay person whose welfare was of special concern to Ven. Sariputta, this discourse teaches two lessons in heedfulness. (1) If you're engaging in wrong livelihood, don't expect to escape the karmic consequences even if you're doing it to fulfil your duties to your family, parents, or friends. (2) Don't be satisfied with mundane levels of attainment in meditation when there is still more to be done.

documents.title: Devadaha Sutta
documents.subtitle: At Devadaha
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha refutes a Jain theory of kamma, which claims that one's present experience is determined solely by one's actions in past lives, and that the effects of past unskillful actions can be "burned away" through austerity practices. The Buddha here outlines one of his most important teachings on kamma: that it is both the results of past deeds and present actions that shape one's experience of the present. It is precisely this interaction of present and past that opens up the very possibility of Awakening.

documents.title: Sunakkhatta Sutta
documents.subtitle: To Sunakkhatta
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha addresses the problem of meditators who overestimate their progress in meditation. The sutta ends with a warning: anyone who claims enlightenment as license for unrestrained behavior is like someone who fails to follow the doctor's orders after surgery, who knowingly drinks a cup of poison, or who deliberately extends a hand toward a deadly snake.

documents.title: Aneñja-sappaya Sutta
documents.subtitle: Conducive to the Imperturbable
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Advanced meditation instruction: how the fourth jhana and the formless attainments can be developed and used as a basis for the realization of Nibbana.

documents.title: Ganakamoggallana Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Discourse to Ganaka-Moggallana
documents.author: I.B. Horner
documents.summary: The Buddha sets forth the gradual training of the Buddhist monk and describes himself as a "shower of the way."

documents.title: Gopaka Moggallana Sutta
documents.subtitle: Moggallana the Guardsman
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Ven. Ananda explains how the Sangha maintains its unity and internal discipline after the passing away of the Buddha [BB]. Interestingly, this sutta also shows that early Buddhist practice had no room for many practices that developed in later Buddhist traditions, such as appointed lineage holders, elected ecclesiastical heads, or the use of mental defilements as a basis for concentration practice.

documents.title: Maha-punnama Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Great Full-moon Night Discourse
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: A thorough discussion of issues related to the five aggregates. Toward the end of the discussion, a monk thinks that he has found a loophole in the teaching. The way the Buddha handles this incident shows the proper use of the teachings on the aggregates: not as a metaphysical theory, but as a tool for questioning clinging and so gaining release.

documents.title: Cula-punnama Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Shorter Discourse on the Full-moon Night
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: How to recognize — and become — a person of integrity.

documents.title: Anupada Sutta
documents.subtitle: One After Another
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: A description of how insight can be developed either while in, or immediately after withdrawing from, the different levels of jhana.

documents.title: Sappurisa Sutta
documents.subtitle: A Person of Integrity
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: What is a person of integrity?

documents.title: Isigili Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Discourse at Isigili
documents.author: Piyadassi Thera
documents.summary: The Buddha enumerates the many paccekabuddhas who lived on Isigili mountain.

documents.title: Maha-cattarisaka Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Great Forty
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: On the nature of noble right concentration, and its interdependence with all the factors of the noble eightfold path.

documents.title: Anapanasati Sutta
documents.subtitle: Mindfulness of Breathing
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: One of the most important texts for beginning and veteran meditators alike, this sutta is the Buddha's roadmap to the entire course of meditation practice, using the vehicle of breath meditation. The simple practice of mindfulness of breathing leads the practitioner gradually through 16 successive phases of development, culminating in full Awakening.

documents.title: Kayagata-sati Sutta
documents.subtitle: Mindfulness Immersed in the Body
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: This sutta serves as a companion to the Anapanasati Sutta, and explains the importance of establishing a broad awareness of the body in meditation to develop jhana.

documents.title: Cula-suññata Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Lesser Discourse on Emptiness
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha instructs Ven. Ananda on the practice that leads to the "entry into emptiness," the doorway to liberation.

documents.title: Maha-suññata Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Greater Discourse on Emptiness
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha instructs Ananda on several practical aspects of the meditative dwelling in emptiness, a mode of awareness that can ultimately bring the meditator to the threshold of Awakening.

documents.title: Dantabhumi Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Discourse on the 'Tamed Stage'
documents.author: I.B. Horner
documents.summary: By analogy with the taming of an elephant, the Buddha explains how he tames his disciples.

documents.title: Bhumija Sutta
documents.subtitle: To Bhumija
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Does the desire for Awakening get in the way of Awakening? According to this discourse, the question of desiring or not desiring is irrelevant as long as one develops the appropriate qualities that constitute the path to Awakening. The discourse is also very clear on the point that there are right and wrong paths of practice: as a geographer might say, not every river flows to the sea.

documents.title: Devaduta Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Deva Messengers
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha's eyewitness account of hell.

documents.title: Bhaddekaratta Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Discourse on the Ideal Lover of Solitude
documents.author: Bhikkhu Ñanananda
documents.summary: In this stirring discourse the Buddha underscores the vital urgency of keeping one's attention firmly rooted in the present moment. After all, the past is gone, the future isn't here; this present moment is all we have.

documents.title: Cula-kammavibhanga Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Shorter Analysis of Action
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Why do some people live a long life, but others die young? Why are some people born poor, but others born rich? The Buddha explains how kamma accounts for a person's fortune or misfortune.

documents.title: Maha Kammavibhanga Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Great Exposition of Kamma
documents.author: Ñanamoli Thera
documents.summary: Two lessons in the dangers of quick generalization. In the first, the Buddha points out that the perception of all feeling as stressful is not appropriate at all stages of the practice. In the second, he shows that generalizing too quickly on the basis of what one sees in meditation can lead to serious wrong view.

documents.title: Maha Kammavibhanga Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Greater Analysis of Action
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Two lessons in the dangers of quick generalization. In the first, the Buddha points out that the perception of all feeling as stressful is not appropriate at all stages of the practice. In the second, he shows that generalizing too quickly on the basis of what one sees in meditation can lead to serious wrong view.

documents.title: Salayatana-vibhanga Sutta
documents.subtitle: An Analysis of the Six Sense-media
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: A discussion of the emotions: where they come from, how they function in the path of practice, and how they manifest in an awakened person who is fit to teach others.

documents.title: Uddesa-vibhanga Sutta
documents.subtitle: An Analysis of the Statement
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: How to attend to outside objects without letting the mind become externally scattered, and how to focus in strong states of absorption without becoming internally positioned. It's not easy, but it can be done.

documents.title: Dhatu-vibhanga Sutta
documents.subtitle: An Analysis of the Properties
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: A poignant story in which a wanderer, searching for the Buddha, meets the Buddha without realizing it. He recognizes his mistake only after the Buddha teaches him a profound discourse on four determinations and the six properties of experience. An excellent illustration of the Buddha's statement, "Whoever sees the Dhamma sees me."

documents.title: Saccavibhanga Sutta
documents.subtitle: Discourse on The Analysis of the Truths
documents.author: Piyadassi Thera

documents.title: Saccavibhanga Sutta
documents.subtitle: An Analysis of the Truths
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Ven. Sariputta gives a detailed elaboration on the Buddha's teaching of the Four Noble Truths.

documents.title: Anathapindikovada Sutta
documents.subtitle: Instructions to Anathapindika
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Ven. Sariputta offers a deep teaching on non-clinging to the ailing lay-follower Anathapindika.

documents.title: Nandakovada Sutta
documents.subtitle: Nandaka's Exhortation
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Ven. Nandaka discusses impermanence with a large group of nuns, driving his point home with particularly vivid similes. It was an effective teaching: soon afterwards, these nuns all become enlightened.

documents.title: Cula-Rahulovada Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Shorter Exposition to Rahula
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha leads his son, Ven. Rahula, to arahantship.

documents.title: Chachakka Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Six Sextets
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: How the contemplation of the six senses leads to an understanding of not-self and, ultimately, to Awakening.

documents.title: Maha-salayatanika Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Great Six Sense-media Discourse
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: How a clear understanding of the six senses leads to the development of the Wings to Awakening and to final release.

documents.title: Indriya-bhavana Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Development of the Faculties
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: What qualifies as full mastery of the senses?

documents.title: Ogha-tarana Sutta
documents.subtitle: Crossing over the Flood
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha explains how he "crossed over the flood" of craving.

documents.title: Arañña Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Wilderness
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Why do monks living in the forest wilderness look so happy?

documents.title: Hiri Sutta
documents.subtitle: Conscience
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: A lovely short teaching on the rarity and value of conscientiousness.

documents.title: Samiddhi Sutta
documents.subtitle: About Samiddhi
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: A devata wonders: why waste time meditating in the hopes of some future reward, when one can enjoy sensual pleasures right here and now?

documents.title: Sakalika Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Stone Sliver
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: After an attempt on his life, the Buddha shows by example how to handle pain.

documents.title: Aditta Sutta
documents.subtitle: (The House) On Fire
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: A deva visits the Buddha and recites a lovely verse on the urgency of giving.

documents.title: Kindada Sutta
documents.subtitle: A Giver of What
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha explains to a deva the true value of various kinds of gifts.

documents.title: Iccha Sutta
documents.subtitle: Desire
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: A brief and elegant summary of the heart of the Buddha's teaching.

documents.title: Ghatva Sutta
documents.subtitle: Having Killed
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha describes one thing that deserves to be killed.

documents.title: Kamada Sutta
documents.subtitle: Kamada's Lament
documents.author: Andrew Olendzki
documents.summary: The Buddha reassures a doubting deva that, though the journey to Awakening may indeed be long and hard, it really can be done.

documents.title: Pañcalacanda Sutta
documents.subtitle: Pañcalacanda the Deva's Son
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Finding an opening to Nibbana.

documents.title: Candima Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Moon Deity's Prayer for Protection
documents.author: Piyadassi Thera
documents.summary: The Buddha intervenes on behalf of a deva caught in the grips of an evil demon. This sutta belongs to the group of paritta (protection) suttas that are chanted by monastics for devotional and ceremonial purposes.

documents.title: Suriya Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Sun Deity's Prayer for Protection
documents.author: Piyadassi Thera
documents.summary: The Buddha intervenes on behalf of a deva caught in the grips of an evil demon. This sutta belongs to the group of paritta (protection) suttas that are chanted by monastics for devotional and ceremonial purposes.

documents.title: Uttara Sutta
documents.subtitle: Uttara the Deva's Son
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Doing good and making merit: are these the best one can aim for in this short life?

documents.title: Rohitassa Sutta
documents.subtitle: To Rohitassa
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: A well-traveled deva learns that we don't have to go to the ends of the world to find an end to suffering; we need look no further than right here, in this very body.

documents.title: Dahara Sutta
documents.subtitle: Young
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha reminds King Pasenadi that one's age is no measure of one's wisdom.

documents.title: Piya Sutta
documents.subtitle: Dear
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: If you truly care about your own and others' welfare, then choose your actions with care.

documents.title: Atta-rakkhita Sutta
documents.subtitle: Self-protected
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha's defense policy.

documents.title: Appaka Sutta
documents.subtitle: Few
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha reminds King Pasenadi of the pitfalls of wealth and luxury.

documents.title: Atthakarana Sutta
documents.subtitle: In Judgment
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: King Pasenadi discovers what motivates people to tell lies.

documents.title: Donapaka Sutta
documents.subtitle: King Pasenadi Goes on a Diet
documents.author: Andrew Olendzki
documents.summary: How King Pasenadi learns to use mindfulness to control his overeating

documents.title: Sangama Sutta
documents.subtitle: A Battle (2)
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Two stories about the battles fought between rival kings, poignantly demonstrating how in war there is security neither for the victor nor the vanquished.

documents.title: Appamada Sutta
documents.subtitle: Heedfulness
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha reveals the one quality in the heart that offers real security.

documents.title: Aputtaka Sutta
documents.subtitle: Heirless (1)
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha advises a rich householder on the proper use and enjoyment of wealth.

documents.title: Aputtaka Sutta
documents.subtitle: Heirless (2)
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Give generously and without regret, or you may suffer the same sad consequences as this wealthy householder.

documents.title: Loka Sutta
documents.subtitle: (Qualities of) the World
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Three common things in the world that inevitably lead to harm and suffering.

documents.title: Issattha Sutta
documents.subtitle: Archery Skills
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Generosity yields good results. But to whom should we give to reap the very best results?

documents.title: Pabbatopama Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Simile of the Mountains
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha offers a powerful simile to King Pasenadi to underscore the imminence of death and the urgency of Dhamma practice.

documents.title: Nandana Sutta
documents.subtitle: Delight
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Mara and the Buddha debate this question: Are possessions a source of joy or of grief?

documents.title: Sakalika Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Stone Sliver
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha, recuperating from an assassination attempt, receives an unwelcome visit from Mara.

documents.title: Kassaka Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Farmer
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Mara proclaims his dominion over the sensory world, but the Buddha explains that he (Buddha) dwells in the one place that Mara can never go.

documents.title: Rajja Sutta
documents.subtitle: Rulership
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Mara, seeing that the Buddha has developed the four bases of power (iddhipada), tries to persuade him to give up the monastic life and become a powerful world ruler.

documents.title: Alavika Sutta
documents.subtitle: Sister Alavika
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Mara: Why bother meditating? Why not just relax and enjoy life's pleasures?

documents.title: Soma Sutta
documents.subtitle: Sister Soma
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Can women achieve Awakening? Ven. Sister Soma handles this misguided question with ease.

documents.title: Gotami Sutta
documents.subtitle: Sister Gotami
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Mara: Why bother sitting in solitude in the forest?

documents.title: Vijaya Sutta
documents.subtitle: Sister Vijaya
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Mara: Why don't we just put aside the meditation for awhile and go out dancing?

documents.title: Uppalavanna Sutta
documents.subtitle: Sister Uppalavanna
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Mara: Why don't you just give up the dangers of the forest and live somewhere safer?

documents.title: Cala Sutta
documents.subtitle: Sister Cala
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Mara: What's wrong with being reborn, anyway?

documents.title: Upacala Sutta
documents.subtitle: Sister Upacala
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Mara: Why not just settle for a happy rebirth among the devas?

documents.title: Sisupacala Sutta
documents.subtitle: Sister Sisupacala
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Sister Sisupacala shows Mara how following the path of Dhamma doesn't mean buying into to a fixed philosophy.

documents.title: Sela Sutta
documents.subtitle: Sister Sela
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Mara tries to trip up Ven. Sister Sela with metaphysical questions.

documents.title: Vajira Sutta
documents.subtitle: Sister Vajira
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Have you ever found yourself getting lured out of meditation by some fascinating, but utterly speculative, train of thought? Ven. Sister Vajira shows how to deal with this.

documents.title: Ayacana Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Request
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Immediately after his Awakening, the Buddha receives a visit from Brahma Sahampati, who pleads with the Buddha to teach the Dhamma, for the sake of those "with little dust in their eyes."

documents.title: Garava Sutta
documents.subtitle: Reverence
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Shortly after his Awakening, the Buddha reviews the world around him, searching for another being whom he can now rightly call his teacher.

documents.title: Andhakavinda Sutta
documents.subtitle: Let the Wilderness Serve!
documents.author: Andrew Olendzki
documents.summary: Brahma Sahampati offers up verses of praise for the Buddha, who sits meditating alone in the wilderness.

documents.title: Parinibbana Sutta
documents.subtitle: Total Unbinding
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Four eyewitness accounts of the passing away of the Buddha.

documents.title: Akkoso Sutta
documents.subtitle: Abuse
documents.author: Maurice O'Connell Walshe
documents.summary: What is your best response when someone is angry with you? Hint: if you offer some food to a guest, but the guest declines the offer, to whom does the food belong?

documents.title: Jata Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Tangle
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha answers Jata Bharadvaja's famous question, "Who can untangle this tangle [of craving]?"

documents.title: Kasi Bharadvaja Sutta
documents.subtitle: To the Plowing Bharadvaja
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha answers a farmer who asserts that monks do no useful work, and thus don't deserve to eat.

documents.title: Udaya Sutta
documents.subtitle: Breaking the Cycle
documents.author: Andrew Olendzki
documents.summary: In delightfully alliterative Pali verse, the Buddha tells how, without true wisdom, the cycle of death and re-becoming are doomed to drone on and on and on.

documents.title: Maha-sala Sutta
documents.subtitle: Very Rich
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: A touching glimpse into the sorrow that a father feels when his ungrateful children fail to honor him in his old age. Treat your parents well.

documents.title: Navakammika Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Builder
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: What useful work can one possibly accomplish by sitting in meditation under a tree in the forest?

documents.title: Katthaharaka Sutta
documents.subtitle: Firewood-gathering
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: How does the Buddha practice jhana in the forest?

documents.title: Ananda Sutta
documents.subtitle: Ananda
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Ven. Ananda offers advice to Ven. Vangisa on how to subdue lust.

documents.title: Viveka Sutta
documents.subtitle: Seclusion
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: A deva comes to the aid of a forest monk whose mind had been wandering during meditation.

documents.title: Anuruddha Sutta
documents.subtitle: Anuruddha
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: One of Ven. Anuruddha's consorts from a previous life as a deva, visits him and invites him back.

documents.title: Vajjiputta Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Vajjian Princeling
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: If you've ever wondered, "Why bother meditate?", listen to this devata's advice.

documents.title: Ayoniso-manasikara Sutta
documents.subtitle: Inappropriate Attention
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Food for thought for a monk being gnawed away by his thoughts.

documents.title: Gandhatthena Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Thief of a Scent
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Have you ever wished for a guardian angel to warn you before you do something foolish? Here's one with an important lesson.

documents.title: Sudatta Sutta
documents.subtitle: About Sudatta (Anathapindika)
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Anathapindika, the wealthy benefactor who would later donate the famous Jeta's Grove monastery to the Sangha, meets the Buddha for the first time.

documents.title: Alavaka Sutta
documents.subtitle: To the Alavaka Yakkha
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: A yakkha challenges the Buddha with riddles and threatens to beat him up.

documents.title: Dhajagga Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Top of the Standard
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Are you ever overcome by fear? The Buddha offers an antidote.

documents.title: Vepacitti Sutta
documents.subtitle: Calm in the Face of Anger
documents.author: Andrew Olendzki
documents.summary: Sakka, king of the devas, explains to a skeptic how forbearance is the best response to another's anger.

documents.title: Subhasita-jaya Sutta
documents.subtitle: Victory Through What is Well Spoken
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Marvelous account of a debating contest between two deities concerning the best way to respond to an angry person.

documents.title: Paticca-samuppada-vibhanga Sutta
documents.subtitle: Analysis of Dependent Co-arising
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: A summary of the causal chain of dependent co-arising.

documents.title: Mahaa Sakyamuni Gotamo Sutta
documents.subtitle: Gotama the Great Sage of the Sakya
documents.author: Maurice O'Connell Walshe

documents.title: Ahara Sutta
documents.subtitle: Nutriment
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha explains how the teachings on the four nutriments (ahara) fits in with dependent co-arising.

documents.title: Phagguna Sutta
documents.subtitle: To Phagguna
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Questions that presuppose the existence of an abiding "self," are fundamentally invalid. The Buddha shows how to re-frame these questions in a way that conduces to liberation.

documents.title: Kaccaayanagotto Sutta
documents.subtitle: Kaccaayana
documents.author: Maurice O'Connell Walshe
documents.summary: The Buddha explains to Ven. Kaccayana Gotta how dependent co-arising applies in the development of right view.

documents.title: Acela Sutta
documents.subtitle: Naked Kassapa
documents.author: Maurice O'Connell Walshe
documents.summary: A perplexed ascetic asks the Buddha: "Is dukkha created by the self? By other? By both? By neither?" The Buddha's answers at first baffle, then inspire, Kassapa, who eventually gains Awakening.

documents.title: Bala-pandita Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Fool & the Wise Person
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: What is the difference between a fool and a wise person?

documents.title: Paccaya Sutta
documents.subtitle: Requisite Conditions
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha explains that when dependent co-arising is clearly seen and understood, wrong views and confusion disappear.

documents.title: Upanisaa Sutta
documents.subtitle: Upanisaa
documents.author: Maurice O'Connell Walshe
documents.summary: The Buddha explains how seeing deeply into dependent co-arising leads to Awakening. The causal chain here includes an additional set of factors not present in the "standard" chain of dependent co-arising.

documents.title: Bhumija Sutta
documents.subtitle: To Bhumija
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: What is the origin of pleasure and pain? Ven. Sariputta clears up some misconceptions.

documents.title: Bhutamidam Sutta
documents.subtitle: This Has Come Into Being
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: What characterizes the difference between a run-of-the-mill person, one who practices the Dhamma, and one who has fully realized the Dhamma?

documents.title: Avijjapaccaya Sutta
documents.subtitle: From Ignorance as a Requisite Condition
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Is there someone or something that lies behind the processes described in dependent co-arising?

documents.title: Cetanaa Sutta
documents.subtitle: Volition
documents.author: Maurice O'Connell Walshe
documents.summary: The Buddha explains the causal link between mental fabrications and consciousness.

documents.title: Loka Sutta
documents.subtitle: The World
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: How the world arises and falls according to the law of dependent co-arising.

documents.title: Aññatra Sutta
documents.subtitle: A Certain Brahman
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: A brahman wonders: When I perform an action (kamma), am I the same person when I experience its results, or am I a different person? The Buddha helps to clear up this man's confused thinking.

documents.title: Lokayatika Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Cosmologist
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Oneness of all being is sometimes taught as a basic Buddhist principle, but this discourse shows that the Buddha himself rejected the idea. It is simply one of the extremes that he avoided by teaching dependent co-arising.

documents.title: Upadana Sutta
documents.subtitle: Clinging
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha uses a marvelous fire simile to describe the nature of clinging.

documents.title: Assutavā Sutta
documents.subtitle: Uninstructed (1)
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: With a striking simile, the Buddha points out the folly of believing this fickle mind to be "self."

documents.title: Puttamansa Sutta
documents.subtitle: A Son's Flesh
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: A meditation on inter-relatedness, showing with four striking similes the suffering inherent in everything the body and mind depend upon for nourishment.

documents.title: Atthi Raga Sutta
documents.subtitle: Where There is Passion
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha describes four factors to which the mind habitually clings. Those who succeed in abandoning passion for these "nutriments" can realize the cessation of birth, aging, and death.

documents.title: Nagara Sutta
documents.subtitle: The City
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha retells the story of how, on the eve of his Awakening, he re-discovered the long-forgotten laws of dependent co-arising and the Four Noble Truths.

documents.title: Nalakalapiyo Sutta
documents.subtitle: Sheaves of Reeds
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: In a discussion about dependent co-arising with Ven. Maha Kotthita, Ven. Sariputta invokes a helpful simile to illustrate the relationship between consciousness and name-and-form.

documents.title: Kosambi Sutta
documents.subtitle: At Kosambi (On Knowing Dependent Co-arising)
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Four good friends share a frank discussion about their grasp of dependent co-arising. One uses a memorable simile to describe the difference between stream-entry and arahatship.

documents.title: Susima Sutta
documents.subtitle: About Susima
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha explains to Susima that development of psychic powers is not a prerequisite for enlightenment.

documents.title: Sattadhatu Sutta
documents.subtitle: Seven Properties
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: An alternative way of looking at the stages of concentration practice

documents.title: Assu Sutta
documents.subtitle: Tears
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: "Which is greater, the tears you have shed while transmigrating and wandering this long, long time... or the water in the four great oceans?"

documents.title: Danda Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Stick
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: We bounce from one birth to the next, as a thrown stick bounces along the ground.

documents.title: Duggata Sutta
documents.subtitle: Fallen on Hard Times
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: When you encounter an unfortunate person, remember: you've been there, too.

documents.title: Sukhita Sutta
documents.subtitle: Happy
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: When you encounter a fortunate person, remember: you've been there, too.

documents.title: Timsa Sutta
documents.subtitle: Thirty
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Which is greater, the blood you have shed in your long journey in samsara, or the water in the four great oceans?

documents.title: Mata Sutta
documents.subtitle: Mother
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: It's hard to meet someone who has not been, at some time in the distant past, your mother, father, son, daughter, sister, or brother.

documents.title: Jinna Sutta
documents.subtitle: Old
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Ven. Maha Kassapa explains why he chooses to continue meditating in the forest wilderness even though he has long since attained arahantship.

documents.title: Saddhammapatirupaka Sutta
documents.subtitle: A Counterfeit of the True Dhamma
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu

documents.title: Saddhamma-pa.tiruupaka.m Sutta
documents.subtitle: False Dhamma
documents.author: Maurice O'Connell Walshe
documents.summary: The Buddha issues a warning: a society that fails to show respect for these five things contributes to the eventual decline and disappearance of the Dhamma.

documents.title: Kumma Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Turtle
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: To seek fame and status: like walking around with a harpoon stuck in your back.

documents.title: Piḷhika Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Dung Beetle
documents.author: Maurice O'Connell Walshe
documents.summary: To seek fame and status: like carrying around a ball of dung.

documents.title: Sigala Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Jackal
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: To seek fame and status is like being a mangy jackal.

documents.title: Nakhasikha Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Tip of the Fingernail
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha offers a simile for the preciousness of this human birth.

documents.title: Satti Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Spear
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Two suttas on the extraordinary power of metta (goodwill).

documents.title: Dhanuggaha Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Archer
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: How quickly life passes! Knowing this, how should we conduct our lives?

documents.title: Ani Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Peg
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Be careful: there are many popular teachings nowadays that may sound good, but they're not necessarily consistent with the Buddha's teachings.

documents.title: Kolita Sutta
documents.subtitle: Kolita
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The real meaning of noble silence.

documents.title: Upatissa Sutta
documents.subtitle: About Upatissa (Sariputta)
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Is there anything in the world whose loss would sadden an arahant?

documents.title: Lakuntaka Bhaddiya Sutta
documents.subtitle: About Bhaddiya the Dwarf
documents.author: K. Nizamis
documents.summary: One's inner wisdom and outward appearance are unrelated.

documents.title: Nando Sutta
documents.subtitle: Nanda
documents.author: Maurice O'Connell Walshe
documents.summary: A primer for monks on what not to wear.

documents.title: Theranama Sutta
documents.subtitle: [A Monk] by the Name of Elder
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha explains to a wandering monk the true meaning of solitude.

documents.title: Nakulapita Sutta
documents.subtitle: To Nakulapita
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha explains to the aging householder Nakulapita how one need not be sick in mind even though one may be sick in body.

documents.title: Devadaha Sutta
documents.subtitle: At Devadaha
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Ven. Sariputta explains the best way to introduce the Buddha's teachings to inquisitive, intelligent people.

documents.title: Haliddakani Sutta
documents.subtitle: To Haliddakani
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Ven. Maha Kaccana explains to a householder what it means to live as a monk, free of society, free of sensual passion, free of yearning, and free of quarreling.

documents.title: Samadhi Sutta
documents.subtitle: Concentration
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: How the development of concentration leads to discernment.

documents.title: Upaadaaparitassanaa Sutta
documents.subtitle: Grasping and Worry
documents.author: Maurice O'Connell Walshe
documents.summary: The Buddha describes how ideas about the self lead to worry, and how to be free of such worry.

documents.title: Bhāra Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Burden
documents.author: Maurice O'Connell Walshe
documents.summary: The Buddha describes the burdens we carry, and how to cast them off.

documents.title: Pariñña Sutta
documents.subtitle: Comprehension
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: True comprehension means the end of passion, aversion, and delusion.

documents.title: Bhikkhu Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Monk
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: How we define ourselves in terms of the aggregates, and how we don't have to do so.

documents.title: Anudhamma Sutta
documents.subtitle: In Accordance with the Dhamma (1)
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Towards the end of his life, the Buddha stated that the proper way to pay homage to him was to "practice the Dhamma in accordance with the Dhamma." This sutta explains what that means, in terms of cultivating disenchantment (nibbida).

documents.title: Anudhamma Sutta
documents.subtitle: In Accordance with the Dhamma (2)
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Towards the end of his life, the Buddha stated that the proper way to pay homage to him was to "practice the Dhamma in accordance with the Dhamma." This sutta explains what that means, in terms of focusing on inconstancy (anicca).

documents.title: Anudhamma Sutta
documents.subtitle: In Accordance with the Dhamma (3)
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Towards the end of his life, the Buddha stated that the proper way to pay homage to him was to "practice the Dhamma in accordance with the Dhamma." This sutta explains what that means, in terms of focusing on stress/suffering (dukkha).

documents.title: Anudhamma Sutta
documents.subtitle: In Accordance with the Dhamma (4)
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Towards the end of his life, the Buddha stated that the proper way to pay homage to him was to "practice the Dhamma in accordance with the Dhamma." This sutta explains what that means, in terms of focusing on not-self (anatta).

documents.title: Attadiipaa Sutta
documents.subtitle: An Island to Oneself
documents.author: Maurice O'Connell Walshe
documents.summary: Presaging the famous words he would utter in his final days, the Buddha elaborates on his advice to "be an island unto yourself."

documents.title: Samanupassanaa Sutta
documents.subtitle: Ways of Regarding
documents.author: Maurice O'Connell Walshe
documents.summary: The Buddha speaks on the assumptions that underlie self-view.

documents.title: Khandha Sutta
documents.subtitle: Aggregates
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha gives a summary of the teaching on the five aggregates.

documents.title: So.no Sutta
documents.subtitle: So.na
documents.author: Maurice O'Connell Walshe
documents.summary: How can you tell when you're seeing things as they really are?

documents.title: Upaya Sutta
documents.subtitle: Attached
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: When passion for each of the five aggregates is completely abandoned, Awakening ensues.

documents.title: Bija Sutta
documents.subtitle: Means of Propagation
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: This sutta is nearly identical to the preceding one (SN 22.53), and illustrates the same point with a striking image.

documents.title: Udana Sutta
documents.subtitle: Exclamation
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: What does it take to break free of the five lower fetters?

documents.title: Parivatta Sutta
documents.subtitle: The (Fourfold) Round
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Awakening results from direct knowledge of the "fourfold round" with respect to the aggregates (i.e., knowledge of the aggregate, its origination, its cessation, and the path leading to its cessation).

documents.title: Sattatthana Sutta
documents.subtitle: Seven Bases
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha explains how one becomes an arahant through mastery of the seven-fold skill of analyzing the five aggregates.

documents.title: Buddha Sutta
documents.subtitle: Awakened
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Some schools of Buddhism teach that there is a qualitative difference between the liberation of a Buddha and that of an arahant disciple — namely, that a Buddha awakens to one level of truth, whereas an arahant awakens to another. This sutta shows that the Buddha saw the distinction in different terms.

documents.title: Anatta-lakkhana Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Discourse on the Not-self Characteristic
documents.author: N.K.G. Mendis

documents.title: Anatta-lakkhana Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Discourse on the Not-self Characteristic
documents.author: Ñanamoli Thera

documents.title: Pañcavaggi Sutta
documents.subtitle: Five Brethren
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha's second discourse, in which he discusses the principle of anatta (not-self) with the group of five ascetics. By means of a question-and-answer dialogue with his audience, the Buddha demonstrates that there can be no abiding self in any of the five aggregates that we tend to identify as "self." The sutta illustrates the Buddha's skillfulness as teacher: at the end of the discourse, all five monks attain full Awakening.

documents.title: Mahali Sutta
documents.subtitle: To Mahali
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha points out that attachment to things comes from paying more attention to the pleasure they give than to the stress and pain (dukkha) they cause. By turning your attention to the dukkha, however, you can gain release.

documents.title: Upaadiyamaano Sutta
documents.subtitle: Clinging
documents.author: Maurice O'Connell Walshe
documents.summary: Cling to anything at all, and you are in bondage to Mara.

documents.title: Khajjaniya Sutta
documents.subtitle: Chewed Up
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: How to gain release from identification with the five aggregates.

documents.title: Pi.n.dolya.m Sutta
documents.subtitle: Going Begging
documents.author: Maurice O'Connell Walshe
documents.summary: A monk who is half-hearted in his meditation misses out on the rewards of both lay life and monastic life.

documents.title: Parileyyaka Sutta
documents.subtitle: At Parileyyaka
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Despite having heard many teachings from the Buddha, a monk still wonders how to bring his meditation practice to a speedy conclusion. The Buddha explains that the goal can be reached by a deep understanding of the five aggregates.

documents.title: Ananda Sutta
documents.subtitle: Ananda
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Ven. Ananda recalls the teachings that led him to stream-entry.

documents.title: Tisso Sutta
documents.subtitle: Tissa the Waverer
documents.author: Maurice O'Connell Walshe
documents.summary: Ven. Tissa, mired in laziness, receives a wake-up call from the Buddha.

documents.title: Yamaka Sutta
documents.subtitle: To Yamaka
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Ven. Yamaka claims that when an arahant dies, he/she is utterly annihilated. Ven. Sariputta pulls him out of this wrong view, and in so doing leads him to Awakening.

documents.title: Anuraadho Sutta
documents.subtitle: Anuraadha is Caught Out
documents.author: Maurice O'Connell Walshe
documents.summary: Ven. Anuradha learns that if you can't even locate the Tathagata in space when he's sitting right in front of you, how can you ever hope to answer questions about his fate after death?

documents.title: Vakkali Sutta
documents.subtitle: Vakkali
documents.author: Maurice O'Connell Walshe
documents.summary: The Buddha gives an ailing Ven. Vakkali a timeless teaching: "He who sees Dhamma, sees me."

documents.title: Khemo Sutta
documents.subtitle: Khemaka
documents.author: Maurice O'Connell Walshe
documents.summary: Although dis-identification with the five aggregates is necessary for becoming a noble disciple, full Awakening calls for even more.

documents.title: Channa Sutta
documents.subtitle: To Channa
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Ven. Channa, formerly the bodhisattha's horseman, receives a teaching on dis-identification with the five aggregates.

documents.title: Nadi Sutta
documents.subtitle: The River
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha explains that a person who incorrectly takes the five aggregates to be "self" is like a person swept away by a swift river, who grasps in vain at the passing trees and branches.

documents.title: Phena Sutta
documents.subtitle: Foam
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha invokes a series of vivid similes to illustrate the voidness of the five aggregates.

documents.title: Nakhasikha Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Tip of the Fingernail
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Not even the slightest trace of the aggregates is exempt from stress and suffering.

documents.title: Gaddula Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Leash (2)
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Those who don't penetrate the not-self nature of the five aggregates are doomed to go round and round in circles, like a dog tied to a post.

documents.title: Nava Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Ship
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha explains that Awakening comes about not by wishful thinking, but only through deliberate effort.

documents.title: Sotaapanno Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Sotaapanna ('Stream-winner')
documents.author: Maurice O'Connell Walshe
documents.summary: What is a Stream-winner?

documents.title: Araha.m Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Arahant
documents.author: Maurice O'Connell Walshe
documents.summary: What is an Arahant?

documents.title: Upadana Sutta
documents.subtitle: Clinging
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: What are the phenomena to which we cling? Answer: each one of the five aggregates.

documents.title: Silavant Sutta
documents.subtitle: Virtuous
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Ven. Sariputta explains how every meditator, from beginner to arahant, should contemplate the five aggregates (khandha).

documents.title: Satta Sutta
documents.subtitle: A Being
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha invokes a dramatic simile to explain how to dismantle attachment to the five aggregates.

documents.title: Cakkhu Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Eye
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: How conviction in, and understanding of, inconstancy of the six senses can lead to stream-entry.

documents.title: Rupa Sutta
documents.subtitle: Forms
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: How conviction in, and understanding of, inconstancy of sense objects can lead to stream-entry.

documents.title: Viññana Sutta
documents.subtitle: Consciousness
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: How conviction in, and understanding of, inconstancy of sense consciousness can lead to stream-entry.

documents.title: Phassa Sutta
documents.subtitle: Contact
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: How conviction in, and understanding of, inconstancy of contact can lead to stream-entry.

documents.title: Vedana Sutta
documents.subtitle: Feeling
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: How conviction in, and understanding of, inconstancy of feeling can lead to stream-entry.

documents.title: Sañña Sutta
documents.subtitle: Perception
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: How conviction in, and understanding of, inconstancy of perception can lead to stream-entry.

documents.title: Cetana Sutta
documents.subtitle: Intention
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: How conviction in, and understanding of, inconstancy of intentions can lead to stream-entry.

documents.title: Tanha Sutta
documents.subtitle: Craving
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: How conviction in, and understanding of, inconstancy of craving can lead to stream-entry.

documents.title: Dhatu Sutta
documents.subtitle: Properties
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: How conviction in, and understanding of, inconstancy of the six elements (earth, liquid, fire, wind, space, and consciousness) can lead to stream-entry.

documents.title: Khandha Sutta
documents.subtitle: Aggregates
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: How conviction in, and understanding of, inconstancy of the five aggregates can lead to stream-entry.

documents.title: Upakkilesa Samyutta
documents.subtitle: Defilements
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: These ten suttas explain why it is worth abandoning desire that is associated with: (1) the six sense bases; (2) their objects; (3) consciousness; (4) contact; (5) feeling; (6) perception; (7) intentions; (8) craving; (9) the six elements (earth, liquid, fire, wind, space, and consciousness); and (10) the five aggregates.

documents.title: Sabba Sutta
documents.subtitle: The All
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: What is the "All"?

documents.title: Pahanaya Sutta
documents.subtitle: To Be Abandoned
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: What, exactly, is it that we must let go of?

documents.title: Adittapariyaya Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Fire Sermon
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Several months after his Awakening, the Buddha delivers this sermon to an audience of 1,000 fire-worshipping ascetics. The Buddha uses the metaphor of fire to illustrate the nature of clinging. Upon hearing the sermon, the entire audience attains full Awakening.

documents.title: Migajaalena Sutta
documents.subtitle: Migajaala
documents.author: Maurice O'Connell Walshe
documents.summary: Why is true solitude so hard to find? The Buddha explains why, no matter where you go, your most annoying companions always tag along.

documents.title: Upasena Sutta
documents.subtitle: Upasena
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Ven. Upasena, mortally wounded by a venomous snake, remains perfectly composed as he utters his dying words to Ven. Sariputta.

documents.title: Gilana Sutta
documents.subtitle: Ill (1)
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: An ailing monk attains stream-entry when the Buddha engages him in a dialogue about not-self.

documents.title: Gilana Sutta
documents.subtitle: Ill (2)
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: An ailing monk attains arahatship when the Buddha engages him in a dialogue about not-self.

documents.title: Avijja Sutta
documents.subtitle: Ignorance
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: What one thing must be abandoned in order to overcome ignorance?

documents.title: Loka Sutta
documents.subtitle: The World
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha explains how all things in the world share one inevitable and unfortunate characteristic. Do you want to remain bound to a world like this?

documents.title: Suñña Sutta
documents.subtitle: Empty
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha explains to Ven. Ananda in what way the world is devoid of anything that can rightly be called "self."

documents.title: Punna Sutta
documents.subtitle: To Punna
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: What would you do with your mind while you're being beaten and stabbed? Consider the Buddha's advice to Punna.

documents.title: Dvaya Sutta
documents.subtitle: A Pair
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: On the arising of sense-consciousness.

documents.title: Maalunkyaputta Sutta
documents.subtitle: Maalunkyaputta
documents.author: Maurice O'Connell Walshe
documents.summary: An aging Ven. Malunkyaputta receives from the Buddha a short teaching regarding dispassion towards the senses ("In reference to the seen, there will be only the seen..."), and soon thereafter becomes an arahant.

documents.title: Pamadaviharin Sutta
documents.subtitle: Dwelling in Heedlessness
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The benefits of living with heedfulness (appamada).

documents.title: Samadhi Sutta
documents.subtitle: Concentration
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha recommends concentration practice as a way to develop discernment.

documents.title: Na Tumhaka Sutta
documents.subtitle: Not Yours
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Do you usually think of "grass" or "leaves" as being "you"? Of course not. In the same way, the sense of "self" cannot be found anywhere within the realm of the senses.

documents.title: Marapasa Sutta
documents.subtitle: Mara's Power
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha explains that once one is completely freed from chasing after sense pleasures, one is then finally safe from Mara.

documents.title: Sariputto Sutta
documents.subtitle: Sariputta
documents.author: Maurice O'Connell Walshe
documents.summary: Ven. Sariputta's advice on how to guard the sense-doors, be moderate in eating, and remain steadfast in heedfulness.

documents.title: Bhaaradvaajo Sutta
documents.subtitle: Bhaaradvaaja Instructs a King
documents.author: Maurice O'Connell Walshe
documents.summary: Ven. Pindola Bharadvaja explains to a king how to maintain one's resolve towards celibacy.

documents.title: Lohicco Sutta
documents.subtitle: Lohicca
documents.author: Maurice O'Connell Walshe
documents.summary: Ven. Mahakaccana's advice on guarding the sense doors.

documents.title: Verahaccaani Sutta
documents.subtitle: Verahaccaani
documents.author: Maurice O'Connell Walshe
documents.summary: For one withholding respect for the Dhamma, Ven. Udayi withholds a Dhamma teaching.

documents.title: Khana Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Opportunity
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: This human realm — neither too pleasurable nor too painful — is the best place to practice Dhamma.

documents.title: Kamma.m Sutta
documents.subtitle: Kamma
documents.author: Maurice O'Connell Walshe
documents.summary: The Buddha explains how the results of "old" kamma (the actions we performed in the past) and "new" kamma (the ones we perform now) are both experienced in the present.

documents.title: Atthinukhopariyaayo Sutta
documents.subtitle: Is There a Criterion?
documents.author: Maurice O'Connell Walshe
documents.summary: The Buddha offers a method for ascertaining whether one has attained enlightenment.

documents.title: Indriya Sutta
documents.subtitle: Faculties
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: A monk asks the Buddha: "What does it mean to be 'consummate in faculties'"?

documents.title: Samuddo (1) Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Ocean (1)
documents.author: Maurice O'Connell Walshe
documents.summary: What does it mean to cross over the ocean of the six senses?

documents.title: Balisika Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Fisherman
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: How to avoid getting caught, like a fish, on Mara's hooks.

documents.title: Ko.t.thiko Sutta
documents.subtitle: Ko.t.thika
documents.author: Maurice O'Connell Walshe
documents.summary: Ven. Sariputta explains to Ven. Maha Kotthita that our problem lies neither in the senses themselves nor in the objects to which the senses cling. Suffering comes from the passion that arises in dependence on both.

documents.title: Udayin Sutta
documents.subtitle: With Udayin
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Since none of the five aggregates can arise on their own, independent of their objects, how can we identify any one of them as "self"?

documents.title: Asivisa Sutta
documents.subtitle: Vipers
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha uses some vivid imagery to illustrate the life-and-death urgency of Dhamma practice.

documents.title: Kumma Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Tortoise
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: If we guard the senses wisely, as a tortoise guards against attack by withdrawing into the safety of its shell, we are safely out of Mara's reach.

documents.title: Daruka-khandha Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Log
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: A lowly cowherd overhears the Buddha speak of the many hazards that lurk in the stream to Nibbana. He takes it to heart and soon succeeds in reaching the goal.

documents.title: Avassuta Sutta
documents.subtitle: Soggy
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: How to guard your concentration against Mara's onslaughts.

documents.title: Dukkhadhammaa Sutta
documents.subtitle: Things Productive of Suffering
documents.author: Maurice O'Connell Walshe
documents.summary: One practiced in sense restraint dispels evil states just as water drops evaporate from a hot iron pot.

documents.title: Ki.msukaa Sutta
documents.subtitle: The 'What's It' Tree (Ki.msuka)
documents.author: Maurice O'Connell Walshe
documents.summary: The Buddha explains how tranquillity (samatha) and insight (vipassana) function together as a "swift pair of messengers" to guide the meditator onwards to Nibbana.

documents.title: Vii.naa Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Lute
documents.author: Maurice O'Connell Walshe
documents.summary: The heart of insight (vipassana): When you take apart a lute in search of its music, what do you find? When you take apart the five aggregates in search of "self," what do you find?

documents.title: Chapaa.na Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Six Animals
documents.author: Maurice O'Connell Walshe
documents.summary: The Buddha explains how training the mind is like keeping six unruly animals tied together on a leash.

documents.title: Yavakalapi Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Sheaf of Barley
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: This sutta, though disjointed, offers some fine similes to illustrate the mind's tendency to create suffering for itself.

documents.title: Samadhi Sutta
documents.subtitle: Concentration
documents.author: Nyanaponika Thera
documents.summary: How an understanding of feeling leads to Nibbana.

documents.title: Sukha Sutta
documents.subtitle: Happiness
documents.author: Nyanaponika Thera
documents.summary: How an understanding of feeling leads to the ending of passion.

documents.title: Pahana Sutta
documents.subtitle: Giving Up
documents.author: Nyanaponika Thera
documents.summary: True freedom is found by abandoning the mind's underlying habitual tendencies (anusaya).

documents.title: Patala Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Bottomless Chasm
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha teaches that by meeting intense physical pain with mindfulness, we can spare ourselves from falling into a bottomless pit of anguish and suffering.

documents.title: Datthabba Sutta
documents.subtitle: To Be Known
documents.author: Nyanaponika Thera
documents.summary: Behind even the happiest and most pleasant of feelings lurks a persistent pain that can, through practice, be overcome.

documents.title: Sallatha Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Arrow
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: When shot by the arrow of physical pain, an unwise person makes matters worse by piling mental anguish on top of it, just as if he had been shot by two arrows. A wise person feels the sting of one arrow alone.

documents.title: Gelañña Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Sick Ward (1)
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha visits a sick ward, and offers advice to the monks on how to approach death with mindfulness.

documents.title: Gelañña Sutta
documents.subtitle: At the Sick Room (2)
documents.author: Nyanaponika Thera
documents.summary: (This sutta is nearly identical to the preceding one, except here the feeling of pleasure, etc., is said to be dependent on contact rather than on the body.)

documents.title: Anicca Sutta
documents.subtitle: Impermanent
documents.author: Nyanaponika Thera
documents.summary: The impermanence of feeling.

documents.title: Phassamulaka Sutta
documents.subtitle: Rooted in Sense-impression
documents.author: Nyanaponika Thera
documents.summary: How sense-impression gives rise to feeling.

documents.title: Rahogata Sutta
documents.subtitle: Alone
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha explains how the practice of jhāna leads to progressive stages of cessation and stillness. Only when the defilements are finally extinguished, however, is true peace and stillness achieved.

documents.title: Akasa Sutta
documents.subtitle: In the Sky (1)
documents.author: Nyanaponika Thera
documents.summary: Feelings rise and fall, like winds blowing across the skies.

documents.title: Agara Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Guest House
documents.author: Nyanaponika Thera
documents.summary: Feelings come and go, like house-guests.

documents.title: Santaka Sutta
documents.subtitle: To Ananda (1)
documents.author: Nyanaponika Thera
documents.summary: The Buddha explains to Ven. Ananda the origin of, danger in, and escape from feeling.

documents.title: Pañcakanga Sutta
documents.subtitle: With Pañcakanga
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha describes the many kinds of happiness that can be experienced through sustained practice. Which kind of happiness do you seek?

documents.title: Sivaka Sutta
documents.subtitle: To Sivaka
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha explains that present experience cannot be described solely in terms of the results of past actions (kamma).

documents.title: Atthasata Sutta
documents.subtitle: The One-hundred-and-eight Exposition
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: A summary and enumeration of the different ways that the Buddha has analyzed feeling (hint: 3x6x6=108).

documents.title: Bhikkhu Sutta
documents.subtitle: To a Certain Bhikkhu
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: A discussion of the ways that feeling must be understood in order to gain freedom from attachment to feeling. Includes an interesting reference to craving as an unskillful "path of practice."

documents.title: Niramisa Sutta
documents.subtitle: Not of the Flesh
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha describes the various grades of potential happiness and freedom, ranging from the worldly to the transcendent.

documents.title: Vaddha Sutta
documents.subtitle: Growth
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: This brief sutta, which encourages education for women, may account for the fact that in the pre-modern world Theravada Buddhist countries had the highest rates of female literacy.

documents.title: Dukkha Sutta
documents.subtitle: Stress
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Ven. Sariputta describes three kinds of stress (dukkha) and how they should be comprehended.

documents.title: Animitto Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Signless
documents.author: Maurice O'Connell Walshe
documents.summary: Ven. Moggallana describes the concentration attainment beyond the eighth jhana.

documents.title: Isidatta Sutta
documents.subtitle: About Isidatta
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: On the origin of self-view. In this touching story, Ven. Isidatta, a wise young forest monk, declines his elders' invitation to become a Dhamma teacher, and instead quietly slips off into the forest and disappears.

documents.title: Mahaka Sutta
documents.subtitle: About Mahaka
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: How one monk misused his psychic powers.

documents.title: Pathama Kamabhu
documents.subtitle: About Kamabhu (1)
documents.author: K. Nizamis
documents.summary: Citta the householder explains to a monk the meaning of a metaphorical verse previously uttered by the Buddha.

documents.title: Kamabhu Sutta
documents.subtitle: With Kamabhu (2)
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Ven. Kamabhu answers a layperson's detailed questions concerning cessation-attainment (nirodha-samapatti), a state of profound — and potentially liberating — concentration whose prerequisite is full mastery of the jhanas.

documents.title: Godatta Sutta
documents.subtitle: To Godatta
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: A layperson points out the similarities and differences between several states of concentration.

documents.title: Gilaana-dassana.m Sutta
documents.subtitle: Seeing the Sick (Citta)
documents.author: Maurice O'Connell Walshe
documents.summary: While on his deathbed, Citta delivers an inspiring teaching on generosity to his friends, his family, and a gathering of devas.

documents.title: Talaputa Sutta
documents.subtitle: To Talaputa the Actor
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Comedians and actors take heed: making others laugh may not always be a particularly commendable occupation, as Talaputa learns.

documents.title: Yodhajiva Sutta
documents.subtitle: To Yodhajiva (The Warrior)
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha cautions a soldier against expecting a favorable rebirth because of his battlefield heroics.

documents.title: Paccha-bhumika Sutta
documents.subtitle: [Brahmans] of the Western Land
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha explains how the principles of kamma and rebirth are as inviolable as the law of gravity. Choose your actions with care, lest you sink like a stone!

documents.title: Sankha Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Conch Trumpet
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha clarifies a crucial point about kamma: although you can never undo a past misdeed, there are ways you can mitigate its inevitable harmful results.

documents.title: Kula Sutta
documents.subtitle: Families
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: A questioner challenges the Buddha: "If you're so supportive of familial harmony, then how can you justify accepting alms from poor families in times of famine?"

documents.title: Maniculaka Sutta
documents.subtitle: To Maniculaka
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: A questioner asks the Buddha: "Are monks allowed to use money?"

documents.title: Gandhabhaka (Bhadraka) Sutta
documents.subtitle: To Gandhabhaka (Bhadraka)
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Why do we experience suffering and stress? Using simple analogies, the Buddha offers a clear and penetrating answer.

documents.title: Khema Sutta
documents.subtitle: With Khema
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Ven. Sister Khema explains to King Pasenadi why questions about the fate of the Tathagata after death are unanswerable.

documents.title: Anuradha Sutta
documents.subtitle: To Anuradha
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Ven. Anuradha learns that if one can't even locate the Tathagata in the present life, how can one ever hope to answer questions about his fate after death?

documents.title: Sariputta-Kotthita Sutta
documents.subtitle: Sariputta and Kotthita (1)
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha takes no position on questions about the fate of the Tathagata after death because each question is bound up in the five khandhas.

documents.title: Sariputta-Kotthita Sutta
documents.subtitle: Sariputta and Kotthita (2)
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Questions regarding the fate of the Tathagata do not arise in those who see the aggregates as they actually are.

documents.title: Sariputta-Kotthita Sutta
documents.subtitle: Sariputta and Kotthita (3)
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Questions regarding the fate of the Tathagata do not arise in those who have abandoned passion for the aggregates.

documents.title: Sariputta-Kotthita Sutta
documents.subtitle: Sariputta and Kotthita (4)
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Questions regarding the fate of the Tathagata do not arise in those who no longer take delight in the aggregates, in clinging, in becoming, or in craving.

documents.title: Moggallana Sutta
documents.subtitle: With Moggallana
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha takes no position on the ten speculative views because he does not identify any of the six senses as "self."

documents.title: Vacchagotta Sutta
documents.subtitle: With Vacchagotta
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha takes no position on the ten speculative views because he does not identify any of the five aggregates as "self."

documents.title: Kutuhalasala Sutta
documents.subtitle: With Vacchagotta
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha uses the image of a fire to explain what carries a being over into its next rebirth.

documents.title: Ananda Sutta
documents.subtitle: To Ananda
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Why the Buddha did not take a position on the question of whether or not there is a self.

documents.title: Sabhiya Sutta
documents.subtitle: With Sabhiya
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The fate of the Tathagata after death cannot be described because the causes for any worldly description of his fate would have totally ceased.

documents.title: Avijja Sutta
documents.subtitle: Ignorance
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha explains that ignorance is the cause of wrong view, wrong resolve, wrong speech, etc., whereas clear knowing gives rise to right view and all the factors of the eightfold path.

documents.title: Upaddha Sutta
documents.subtitle: Half (of the Holy Life)
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: In this famous sutta the Buddha corrects Ven. Ananda, pointing out that having "admirable" friends, companions, and comrades is not half but the whole of the holy life. (For more about this special kind of friendship, see the page on kalyanamittata.)

documents.title: Magga-vibhanga Sutta
documents.subtitle: An Analysis of the Path
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: A summary of the Noble Eightfold Path.

documents.title: Agantuka Sutta
documents.subtitle: For All Comers
documents.author: Maurice O'Connell Walshe
documents.summary: The states that are to be comprehended, abandoned, experienced, and cultivated through the practice of the Eightfold Path.

documents.title: Dukkhata Sutta
documents.subtitle: Suffering
documents.author: Maurice O'Connell Walshe
documents.summary: The three kinds of suffering.

documents.title: Ogha Sutta
documents.subtitle: Floods (1)
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Many discourses speak of "crossing over the flood." This discourse lists the floods that should be crossed over, and how it should be done.

documents.title: Himavanta Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Himalayas
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: A summary of the seven Factors for Awakening.

documents.title: Gilana Sutta
documents.subtitle: Ill
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha instructs a very ill Ven. Maha Kassapa on the seven Factors for Awakening.

documents.title: Gilana Sutta
documents.subtitle: Ill
documents.author: Piyadassi Thera
documents.summary: The Buddha, who is very ill, asks Ven. Maha Cunda to recite for him the seven Factors of Awakening.

documents.title: Ahara Sutta
documents.subtitle: Food
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha describes how we can either "feed" or "starve" the wholesome and unwholesome tendencies in the mind according to how we apply our attention.

documents.title: Aggi Sutta
documents.subtitle: Fire
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The right and wrong ways to respond to sluggishness or restlessness.

documents.title: Aggi Sutta
documents.subtitle: Fire
documents.author: Maurice O'Connell Walshe
documents.summary: The right and wrong ways to respond to sluggishness or restlessness.

documents.title: Metta Sutta
documents.subtitle: Good Will
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: How to develop the four brahma-viharas.

documents.title: Mettam Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Brahma-viharas
documents.author: Maurice O'Connell Walshe
documents.summary: How to develop the four brahma-viharas.

documents.title: Sangaravo Sutta
documents.subtitle: Sangarava
documents.author: Maurice O'Connell Walshe
documents.summary: Why do some sacred texts seem clear, while others are muddled?

documents.title: Sakunagghi Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Hawk
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha uses a lovely parable — that of a hawk catching a quail far outside the quail's familiar hunting ground — to reveal the need for keeping the mind in its proper territory: the four frames of reference.

documents.title: Makkata Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Monkey
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Keep your mind in its proper territory — the four frames of reference — lest you lose it altogether, like this pitiful monkey stuck in a tar trap.

documents.title: Suda Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Cook
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: How is meditation like cooking? The Buddha explains.

documents.title: Bhikkhunivasako Sutta
documents.subtitle: Mindfulness
documents.author: Maurice O'Connell Walshe
documents.summary: How to respond skillfully to distracted states of mind that interfere with concentration.

documents.title: Cunda Sutta
documents.subtitle: About Cunda
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Ven. Ananda grieves over Ven. Sariputta's death, and the Buddha consoles him with Dhamma: make the Dhamma your island, your true refuge!

documents.title: Cunda Sutta
documents.subtitle: At Ukkacela
documents.author: Nyanaponika Thera
documents.summary: The Buddha's reaction to the death of Ven. Sariputta.

documents.title: Sedaka Sutta
documents.subtitle: At Sedaka
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Is meditation a selfish endeavor? Using a famous simile of two acrobats, the Buddha resolves this question decisively.

documents.title: Sedaka Sutta
documents.subtitle: At Sedaka
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: How solid is your concentration? Try this test, proposed by the Buddha: Can you keep a glass of oil balanced on your head while your favorite movie star is singing and dancing right in front of you?

documents.title: Sata Sutta
documents.subtitle: Mindful
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: What does it mean to be mindful? What does it mean to be alert?

documents.title: Chanda Sutta
documents.subtitle: Desire
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: How mindfulness leads to freedom from desire — and beyond.

documents.title: Pariñña Sutta
documents.subtitle: Comprehension
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Comprehension of these four things leads to Awakening.

documents.title: Satipatthana-vibhanga Sutta
documents.subtitle: Analysis of the Frames of Reference
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: A summary of the four Frames of Reference, and how they are to be developed.

documents.title: Amata Sutta
documents.subtitle: Deathless
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Don't let the Deathless be lost to you!

documents.title: Samudaya Sutta
documents.subtitle: Origination
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The origination and subsiding of the foundations of mindfulness.

documents.title: Paa.timokkha Sutta
documents.subtitle: Obligation
documents.author: Maurice O'Connell Walshe
documents.summary: Success in meditation depends upon laying skillful foundations.

documents.title: Indriya-vibhanga Sutta
