documents.subtitle: Analysis of the Mental Faculties
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: A summary of the five mental faculties: conviction, persistence, mindfulness, concentration, and discernment.

documents.title: Vibhanga Sutta
documents.subtitle: An Analysis
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: On the Buddha's five-fold classification of feelings.

documents.title: Katthopama Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Fire-stick
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: On the Buddha's five-fold classification of feelings.

documents.title: Jara Sutta
documents.subtitle: Old Age
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha, now a wrinkled old man, issues a stirring rebuke against old age.

documents.title: Pubbakotthaka Sutta
documents.subtitle: Eastern Gatehouse
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha and Ven. Sariputta discuss conviction, and whether it is present in those who have seen the Deathless. They concur that until one experiences the Deathless for oneself, one can only take its existence on faith.

documents.title: Sekha Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Learner
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: How can one tell if one is a "learner" (sekha; one who has attained at least stream-entry, but not yet arahantship) or an arahant?

documents.title: Patitthita Sutta
documents.subtitle: Established
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Heedfulness: the cornerstone upon which all other skillful qualities are based.

documents.title: Brahmana Sutta
documents.subtitle: To Unnabha the Brahman
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Ven. Ananda explains to Unnabha that the path of Dhamma is one with a definite goal — the abandoning of desire — which can only be attained by developing a strong desire to end desire.

documents.title: Iddhipada-vibhanga Sutta
documents.subtitle: Analysis of the Bases of Power
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha explains how the four bases of power are to be developed.

documents.title: Gilana Sutta
documents.subtitle: Illness
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Ven. Anuruddha explains to the other monks how he keeps the pain of his physical illness from invading the mind.

documents.title: Arittha Sutta
documents.subtitle: To Arittha
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha explains that success in meditation calls for more than simply being mindful; there are specific skills that must be developed.

documents.title: Dipa Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Lamp
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: No matter how far along you are in your meditation practice, the basic principle is the same: you should develop and sustain mindfulness of breathing.

documents.title: Vesali Sutta
documents.subtitle: At Vesali
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: How the practice of concentration through mindfulness of breathing clarifies the underlying purpose of other meditation practices.

documents.title: Ananda Sutta
documents.subtitle: To Ananda
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha explains to Ven. Ananda how the sustained practice of mindfulness of breathing (anapanasati) leads, by stages, to full Awakening.

documents.title: Raja Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Emperor
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Which is better: being a world leader, or gaining the four factors of stream-entry?

documents.title: Licchavi Sutta
documents.subtitle: To the Licchavi
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha instructs the layman Nandaka on the four factors of stream-entry.

documents.title: Abhisanda Sutta
documents.subtitle: Bonanzas (3)
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha describes three variations on the four factors of stream-entry.

documents.title: Nandiya Sutta
documents.subtitle: To Nandiya
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: On what it means to live with heedfulness (appamada).

documents.title: Gilana Sutta
documents.subtitle: Ill
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: A short instruction manual on how to give comfort to a gravely ill lay follower.

documents.title: Viggahika Sutta
documents.subtitle: Wordy Warfare
documents.author: Maurice O'Connell Walshe
documents.summary: Some kinds of speech are counterproductive.

documents.title: Dhammacakkappavattana Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Discourse on the Setting in Motion of the Wheel (of Vision) of the Basic Pattern: the Four True Realities for the Spiritually Ennobled Ones
documents.author: Peter Harvey

documents.title: Dhammacakkappavattana Sutta
documents.subtitle: Setting Rolling the Wheel of Truth
documents.author: Ñanamoli Thera

documents.title: Dhammacakkappavattana Sutta
documents.subtitle: Setting in Motion the Wheel of Truth
documents.author: Piyadassi Thera

documents.title: Dhammacakkappavattana Sutta
documents.subtitle: Setting the Wheel of Dhamma in Motion
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: This is the Buddha's first discourse, delivered shortly after his Awakening to the group of five monks with whom he had practiced the austerities in the forest for many years. The sutta contains the essential teachings of the Four Noble Truths and the Noble Eightfold Path. Upon hearing this discourse, the monk Kondañña attains the first stage of Awakening, thus giving birth to the ariya sangha (Noble Sangha).

documents.title: Tatha Sutta
documents.subtitle: Real
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Four things whose fundamental reality makes them worthy of deep contemplation.

documents.title: Simsapa Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Simsapa Leaves
documents.author: Maurice O'Connell Walshe
documents.summary: The Buddha compares the knowledge he gained in his Awakening to all the leaves in the forest, and his teachings to a mere handful of leaves. He then explains why he didn't reveal the remainder.

documents.title: Sattisata Sutta
documents.subtitle: One Hundred Spears
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Life may be fraught with pain, but the breakthrough to Awakening is utterly painless.

documents.title: Pana Sutta
documents.subtitle: Animals
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The path leading out of samsara springs from contemplation of the Four Noble Truths.

documents.title: Papata Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Drop-off
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha reminds an anxious monk who stands with him at the edge of a high cliff that some dangers are far more worrisome than this precipice.

documents.title: Kuta Sutta
documents.subtitle: Gabled
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: Practicing Dhamma is like building a house: you have to start at the bottom and work your way up. You just can't do it the other way round.

documents.title: Vala Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Horsehair
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: It's easy to admire the skill required to master a sport or a feat of manual dexterity. But even more difficult is the skill required to master the Four Noble Truths.

documents.title: Andhakara Sutta
documents.subtitle: Darkness
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: A monk ponders the darkness of deep space and asks the Buddha: "Is there any darkness more frightening than this?" The Buddha asserts that yes, there most certainly is.

documents.title: Chiggala Sutta
documents.subtitle: The Hole
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: The Buddha's famous simile of the blind sea-turtle, illustrating the precious rarity of this human birth.

documents.title: Pansu Suttas
documents.subtitle: Dust
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
documents.summary: A fortunate rebirth is rare. Therefore: practice!

documents.title: Vinaya-samukkamsa
documents.subtitle: The Innate Principles of the Vinaya
documents.author: Thanissaro Bhikkhu
